The constitution of a separate committee of legal professionals or enabling the summons to be issued through a Magistrate, is not warranted (¶¶44-45).Further directions issued by the Supreme Court:(a)  An IO or Station House Officer shall not summon an Advocate appearing for a party to elicit details of the case unless one of the exceptions to Section 132 of the BSA applies. Any such summons must explicitly specify the factual basis of the exception, with the prior written approval of a Superior Officer not below the rank of Superintendent of Police. The summons is subject to judicial review under Section 528 of the BNSS, at the instance of the Advocate or the client. The privilege applies to Advocates engaged in litigation, non-litigious or pre-litigation matters (¶67, directions 1 to 1.4).(b)  Production of documents in the possession of an Advocate or client is not covered by the privilege under Section 132. In criminal cases, production shall be under Section 94 of the BNSS and Section 165 of the BSA; in civil cases, under Section 165 of the BSA and Order XVI Rule 7 of the Code of Civil Procedure. The Court shall hear objections and decide on admissibility (¶67, directions 2 to 2.3).(c)  If a direction to produce a digital device is passed by IO under Section 94 of the BNSS, it shall only be to produce before the Jurisdictional Court. The Court shall hear the party and the Advocate on any objection. If objections are overruled, the device shall be examined in the presence of the party and the Advocate, with digital expert assistance of their choice and discovery shall be confined to what the IO is entitled to seek (¶67, directions 3 to 3.3).(d)  In-house counsel are not entitled to the privilege under Section 132, as they do not practise as independent Advocates. They retain the limited protection under Section 134 of the BSA (¶67, direction 4 to 4.1).The summons in SLP (Crl.) No. 9334 of 2025 was set aside (¶68).Reasons for the Decision:  Attorney-Client Privilege under Section 132 of the BSA1.  The Supreme Court extracted Section 132 of the BSA, noting that it is in pari materia with Section 126 of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872. Section 132 prohibits an Advocate, without the express consent of the client, from disclosing any communication made in the course and for the purpose of his professional service and this obligation continues after the professional engagement has ceased (¶32). The Supreme Court held that the exceptions to Section 132, namely, waiver/consent of the client, communications made in furtherance of an illegal purpose, and facts observed showing that a crime or fraud was committed since the commencement of the engagement, are clear and operate only in those circumstances; the privilege, though conferred on the client, enables the Advocate to invoke it even in the client’s absence; and the complicity of an accused admitted to his lawyer does not fall within the genre of an extra-judicial confession (¶33). The Supreme Court further held, relying on Greenough [39 E.R. 618 (1833)], that the privilege extends to legal advice taken, at a solitary instance, sporadically, on a periodic basis or even under a regular retainership (¶¶34, 53).Absence of a Legislative Vacuum; Jacob Mathew and Vishaka Distinguished2.  The Supreme Court declined to frame guidelines under Article 142 of the Constitution of India. The reliance on Jacob Mathew v. State of Punjab [(2005) 6 SCC 1] was distinguished on the ground that the case concerned professional negligence resulting in criminal liability, particularly in matters of medical negligence, where the alleged professional negligence must, prima facie, be found to exist by another professional of equal or higher competence. In such cases, the nuances of a therapeutic or surgical intervention are alien to an IO or a complainant. The present case concerns not professional misconduct, but the summoning of an advocate to disclose client communications protected under Section 132 of the BSA. (¶¶26-27, 29). Similarly, Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan [AIR 1997 SC 3011] was distinguished as a class action that had addressed a legislative vacuum in the protection of working women against sexual harassment; no comparable vacuum existed in the present case (¶¶28, 30-31). The Court also declined to constitute a pre-summons committee of legal professionals, since a police officer issuing summons under Section 179 of the BNSS is already cautioned by Section 132 and any determination by a committee without the participation of the client would jeopardise the very client whose privilege the measure was intended to protect (¶¶44-45).Right to Legal Representation3.  The Supreme Court held that the right to legal representation is enshrined in the Constitution itself (¶42). Summoning an Advocate to disclose professional communications violates the client’s right against self-incrimination under Article 20(3), since a person cannot be incriminated through the professional communications he made with his counsel in confidence (¶41). The right to effective and adequate legal representation flows from Articles 14, 21, 22(1), and 39-A of the Constitution of India, as confirmed in M.H. Hoskot v. State of Maharashtra [(1978) 3 SCC 544] and Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar [(1980) 1 SCC 98 and 108] and would be jeopardised by summoning a lawyer to be a witness in a case in which he appears for the accused (¶43).Production of Documents and Digital Devices; In-house Counsel4.  The Supreme Court held that the privilege under Section 132 does not extend to the production of documents, relying on Gangaram v. Habib-Ullah [1935 SCC OnLine All 310], Chandubhai Jethabhai Desai v. The State [AIR 1962 Guj 290] and the approval of that by a Three-Judge Bench of this Court in Matter of Great Public Importance Touching upon the Independence of Judiciary [(2019) 19 SCC 405] (¶¶55-57). The production of documents in both criminal and civil proceedings is regulated by the governing procedural framework, with the Court deciding on objections and admissibility (¶¶56, 58). In relation to digital devices, the Supreme Court directed that production shall be only before the Jurisdictional Court, which shall examine the device in the presence of the Advocate, the client and a digital expert of their choice (¶58). In relation to in-house counsel, the Supreme Court held, relying on Rejanish K.V. v. K. Deepa [2025 INSC 1208], that a fully salaried employee does not fall within the definition of an Advocate under the Advocates Act, 1961, read with Rule 49 of the Bar Council of India Rules and therefore cannot claim the privilege under Section 132 of the BSA (¶¶59-62). This position was found to be consistent with Akzo Noble Limited v. European Commission [ECR 2010 I-08301] (Court of Justice of the European Union, Grand Chamber), which held that an in-house lawyer does not enjoy the same degree of independence from his employer as an external lawyer, and that legal professional privilege does not cover exchanges with in-house lawyers (¶¶65-66). In-house counsel retain the limited protection of Section 134 of the BSA (¶67).Prepared by Sahil RatheeCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: SANJABIJ TARI vs KISHORE S.BORCARCrl.A. No. 1755/2010
Subject: Guidelines issued to address the massive backlog of cheque bouncing cases
Judgemental Summary: SANJABIJ TARI V. KISHORE S.BORCAR2025 INSC 1158 (25 September 2025)Justices:  Justice Manmohan and Justice N.V. AnjariaQuestion(s):  Whether a High Court, in its revisional jurisdiction, can overturn concurrent factual findings of lower courts without establishing perversity.Whether the presumptions of a “legally enforceable debt” under Sections 118 and 139 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (“NI Act”) apply when a cash loan exceeds Rs. 20,000/-, violating Section 269SS of the Income Tax Act,1961.Factual Background:  The Appellant-Complainant initiated proceedings under Section 138 of the NI Act against Respondent No. 1-Accused after a cheque was dishonoured. The Trial Court convicted the Accused, and the Sessions Court upheld this conviction, finding that the cheque was given in discharge of a friendly loan of Rs. 6,00,000. The High Court of Bombay, exercising revisional jurisdiction, passed an ex-parte judgment, acquitting the accused and reversing the concurrent judgments of the lower courts. The Appellant approached the Supreme Court challenging this acquittal.Decision of the Supreme Court:  A Two-Judge Bench of the Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the High Court’s ex-parte judgment, and restored the concurrent convictions by the Trial Court and Sessions Court. The Supreme Court directed the Accused to pay Rs. 7,50,000/- in 15 equated monthly instalments of Rs. 50,000/- each. The judgment of the Court was authored by Justice Manmohan.Reasons for the Decision:  Limits of Revisional JurisdictionThe Supreme Court held that in the absence of perversity or a jurisdictional error, it is not open to a High Court exercising revisional jurisdiction to re-analyze and re-interpret evidence and upset the concurrent factual findings of the Trial Court and Sessions Court (¶¶  27, 28)Presumptions under the NI ActThe Supreme Court reiterated that once the execution of a cheque is admitted, strong legal presumptions arise under Sections 118 and 139 of the NI Act that the cheque was drawn for consideration and issued in discharge of a legally enforceable debt. (¶15) The initial burden is on the accused/drawer of cheque to rebut this. (¶17)Defence Found UnbelievableThe Supreme Court agreed with the Sessions Court that the accused’s defence that he had issued a signed blank cheque to enable the complainant to obtain a bank loan was “unbelievable,” as a cheque issued on an account in which there are not sufficient funds could not be used to obtain a bank loan. (¶32)Validity of Cash LoansThe Supreme Court overruled a Kerala High Court judgment in C. Hari v. Shine Varghese (2025 SCC OnLine Ker 5535), and clarified that a cash loan exceeding Rs. 20,000/- does not cease to be a “legally enforceable debt” under the NI Act. A breach of Section 269SS of the Income Tax Act only attracts a tax penalty under Section 271D, it does not render the underlying transaction illegal, invalid, or statutorily void. (¶20)Quasi-Criminal Nature The Supreme Court reiterated that the intent behind introducing Chapter XVII of NI Act is to restore the credibility of cheques as a reliable substitute for cash. By criminalizing the issuance of cheques without sufficient funds or for other specified reasons, the law seeks to ensure financial discipline, deter irresponsible practices, and enable a more efficient and timely resolution of disputes than the earlier purely civil remedy, which often involved prolonged litigation for the payee. (¶14)Revised Compounding RulesIn light of the large number of pending cheque dishonour cases and the reduction in interest rates in recent years, the Supreme Court tweaked its previous compounding guidelines issued in Damodar S. Prabhu v. Sayed Babalal H ((2010) 5 SCC 663), by creating a graded scheme: If the accused pays the cheque amount before the recording of defence evidence, the Trial Court may allow compounding without any cost. If payment is made after defence evidence but before the Trial Court’s judgment, compounding may be permitted on payment of 5% of the cheque amount. If payment is made before the Sessions Court or High Court in revision or appeal, compounding may be allowed on payment of 7.5%; and if tendered before Supreme Court, on payment of 10% of the cheque amount. (¶38)Guidelines for Speedy Disposal of NI Act Cases The Supreme Court issued guidelines to tackle the massive backlog and expedite the disposal of cheque bouncing cases under Section 138 of the NI Act, to be implemented by 01 November 2025. (¶¶36,40) (i) Summons shall also be served through dasti as well as electronic modes such as email or WhatsApp. The complainant shall furnish the accused’s particulars supported by an affidavit.(ii) The complainant shall file an affidavit confirming service of summons. Any false affidavit shall attract appropriate legal action by the Court.(iii) District Courts shall create dedicated, secure online payment links (QR codes/UPI) to be mentioned on the summons, enabling the accused to deposit the cheque amount at the initial stage for immediate compounding and speedy disposal of proceedings.(iv) Every complaint shall include a standardized synopsis, filed immediately after the index, detailing the particulars of the parties, cheque details, reasons for dishonour, statutory notices, and the cause of action.(v) Magistrates are not required to issue summons at the pre-cognizance stage under Section 223 of the BNSS, the NI Act being a special enactment.(vi) Courts shall record cogent reasons before converting a summary trial into a summons trial. The accused’s responses to such questions shall be recorded in the order sheet to determine whether the matter shall proceed as a summary trial.(viii) Trial Courts shall proactively exercise powers under Section 143A of the NI Act to direct interim deposits at the earliest stage.(ix) Upon service of summons, hearings shall ordinarily be conducted in physical courts to facilitate informal interaction and early resolution.(x) High Courts shall prescribe realistic pecuniary limits for evening courts dealing with Section 138 cases to enhance disposal capacity.(xi) District and Sessions Judges in Delhi, Mumbai, and Calcutta shall maintain a dedicated dashboard tracking pendency, disposals, and settlements. Furthermore, they shall conduct monthly reviews, and submit quarterly reports to the High Court.(xii) The Chief Justices of the Delhi, Bombay, and Calcutta High Courts are requested to constitute administrative committees to monitor pendency and to ensure expeditious disposal of Section 138 of the NI Act cases. These committees shall meet monthly, and promote Alternative Dispute Resolution mechanisms.Prepared by Nikhil Kumar ChaubeyCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: ANJUMAN ISHAAT E TALEEM TRUST vs THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRAC.A. No. 1385/2025
Subject: Mandatory nature of Teacher Eligibility Test in minority institutions and the scope of their exemption under the RTE Act
Judgemental Summary: ANJUMAN ISHAAT E TALEEM TRUST V. THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA2025 INSC 1063 (1 September 2025)Justices:  Justice Dipankar Datta and Justice ManmohanQuestion(s):  The Supreme Court decided the following questions arising in a consolidated batch of civil appeals filed by minority educational institutions, State education authorities, and in-service teachers:1.  Whether the State can insist that a teacher seeking appointment in a minority educational institution must qualify the Teacher Eligibility Test (“TET”), and if so, whether providing such a qualification would affect any of the rights of the minority institutions guaranteed under the Constitution of India.2.  Whether teachers appointed prior to the issuance of the National Council for Teacher Education (“NCTE”) Notification dated 29 July 2011, who have years of teaching experience, are required to qualify in the TET for being considered eligible for promotion.In the course of analysis, the Court also considered whether the Constitution Bench decision in Pramati Educational and Cultural Trust v. Union of India [(2014) 8 SCC 1], which exempted all minority educational institutions, aided or unaided, from the purview of the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009 (“RTE Act”), warranted reconsideration by a larger bench.Factual Background:  The appeals challenged judgments of the Bombay and Madras High Courts arising from a consolidated batch of civil appeals filed by three categories of appellants: minority educational institutions aggrieved that they were not being allowed to recruit teachers who had not qualified the TET; State authorities claiming that TET qualification is mandatory for appointment of teachers in both non-minority and minority institutions; and individual in-service teachers claiming that the TET cannot be made mandatory for their promotion. The lead appeal arose from a Bombay High Court judgment dated 12 December 2017, which upheld a Government Resolution of Maharashtra making TET qualification a pre-condition for appointment of teachers in primary schools. Other appeals arose from judgments of the Madras High Court which held the TET mandatory for teachers in non-minority institutions, relied on Pramati Educational and Cultural Trust to hold it inapplicable to minority institutions, and also held TET mandatory for promotion.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The judgment was authored by Justice Dipankar Datta. On the question of the RTE Act’s applicability to minority institutions, the Court expressed doubt about the correctness of Pramati Educational and Cultural Trust and referred questions to the Chief Justice of India for consideration by a larger bench (¶¶208–210). Pending the reference and subject to the answers to the questions formulated for the larger bench, the RTE Act continues to apply to all schools as defined in Section 2(n) except those established and administered by minorities, till such time the reference is decided (¶214). Civil Appeal Nos. 1364–1367, 1385–1386, 6364, and 6365–6367 of 2025 were accordingly directed to be placed before the Chief Justice for appropriate directions (¶¶211–213). On the question of the TET’s applicability to in-service teachers of non-minority schools, the Court disposed of Civil Appeal Nos. 1389, 1390, 1391, 1393, 1395–1399, 1401, 1403, and 1404–1410 of 2025 with modifications to the impugned judgments. The TET was held mandatory for both initial appointment and promotion. Invoking Article 142, teachers having less than five years of service left as on date were permitted to continue in service without the TET qualification until superannuation, but may not be considered for promotion without it; teachers with more than five years remaining must qualify the TET within two years, failing which they shall be compulsorily retired with terminal benefits (¶¶214–219).Reasons for the Decision:  The Constitutional Journey of Article 21A and the RTE Act1.  The right to elementary education, though initially recognised only as a Directive Principle under Article 45 of the Constitution, was progressively elevated to a fundamental right through judicial interpretation in Mohini Jain v. State of Karnataka [(1992) 3 SCC 666] and Unni Krishnan, J.P. v. State of Andhra Pradesh [(1993) 1 SCC 645], culminating in its codification as Article 21A by the Constitution (Eighty-sixth Amendment) Act, 2002 (¶¶72–82). Parliament consciously placed Article 21A in the “Right to Freedom” chapter of Part III and not in the “Cultural and Educational Rights” chapter where Article 30 is located (¶171). The RTE Act, enacted to give effect to Article 21A, lays down the bare minimum core obligations and standards that all schools must follow, including trained teachers, a prescribed student-teacher ratio, adequate infrastructure, and inclusive admission policies (¶87). Section 12(1)(c) of the RTE Act, which mandates a 25% reservation at the entry level for children belonging to weaker sections and disadvantaged groups from the neighbourhood, was described as perhaps the closest the nation has come to realising the vision of an inclusive and rights-based universal elementary education (¶102). Quality of education is inherent in the right under Article 21A, and compromising the quality of teachers necessarily compromises the right of children to quality education (¶163).Article 30(1) as a Non-Absolute Right and the RTE Act as a Reasonable Restriction2.  Article 30(1) guarantees minorities the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice, but this right has never been construed as conferring blanket immunity from all forms of regulation (¶135). A seven-Judge Bench in In Re: Kerala Education Bill, 1957 [1959 SCR 995] held that a minority institution does not shed its character merely by admitting a “sprinkling of outsiders” (¶140). A nine-Judge Bench in Ahmedabad St. Xavier’s College Society v. State of Gujarat [(1974) 1 SCC 717], citing Das CJ in Kerala Education Bill, reaffirmed that the right to administer is not the right to mal-administer (¶150). An eleven-Judge Constitution Bench in T.M.A. Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka [(2002) 8 SCC 481] overruled an earlier view and confirmed that any regulation framed in the national interest must necessarily apply to all educational institutions, whether run by the majority or the minority, and that the right under Article 30(1) cannot override the national interest (¶148). The RTE Act, enacted to give effect to Article 21A, is a reasonable restriction falling within the contours of Article 19(6), aimed at advancing universal elementary education: its regulatory measures are not arbitrary but are necessary, imperative, and proportionate (¶146). Rights under Article 30(1), not being absolute, cannot be claimed to the complete exclusion of Article 21A; both must co-exist harmoniously (¶151).Doubting Pramati Educational and Cultural Trust: The Case for Reference3.  The Court expressed doubt about the correctness of the Constitution Bench decision in Pramati Educational and Cultural Trust on several grounds. First, the Bench in Pramati Educational and Cultural Trust focused solely on Section 12(1)(c) of the RTE Act and offered no analysis of any other provision of the RTE Act vis-à-vis Article 30(1), yet granted a sweeping blanket exemption from the entire RTE Act without articulating reasons for extending the exemption beyond Section 12(1)(c) (¶¶120–121). Second, in relation to aided minority institutions, Article 29(2) of the Constitution, which expressly prohibits denial of admission in State-aided educational institutions on grounds of religion, race, caste, or language, was not considered (¶141). Third, even the concern underlying Section 12(1)(c) could have been resolved by reading it down: the 25% quota does not mandate that children admitted must belong to a different religious or linguistic community; disadvantaged children from the minority community itself could be admitted to fulfil the requirement without any dilution of institutional identity (¶¶180–183). Fourth, the practical consequences of the exemption were found to be deeply troubling: only 8.76% of students in minority schools belong to socially and economically disadvantaged sections; approximately 85% of institutions received minority status post-2006, many after the RTE Act’s enactment; and children in minority schools are denied the range of statutory entitlements that the RTE Act affords (¶¶115, 125, 127). The Court accordingly referred four questions to the Chief Justice of India for consideration by a larger bench, including whether Pramati Educational and Cultural Trust was correctly decided and whether Section 12(1)(c) should have been read down rather than declared ultra vires (¶210).The TET as a Mandatory Minimum Qualification4.  The TET was held to be a mandatory minimum qualification under Section 23 of the RTE Act (¶204), which authorises the Central Government to designate an academic authority to lay down minimum qualifications for teachers; the NCTE was so designated and issued its Notification dated 23 August 2010 making the TET mandatory for appointment of teachers in classes I to VIII in schools covered by the RTE Act (¶¶60–62). Subsequent notifications retained the mandatory TET requirement unchanged (¶63). The Court found no meaningful distinction between a “minimum qualification” and an “eligibility criterion” in this context: a person must qualify the TET to become eligible for appointment as a teacher, and the two expressions are synonymous in their legal consequence (¶¶204–206). Applying the TET only to non-minority institutions and not to minority institutions would amount to an impermissible classification under Article 14, as it would create differential eligibility criteria based solely on the religious or linguistic character of an institution, further violating the general right under Article 21A (¶191).Applicability of TET to Promotions and In-Service Teachers5.  The term “appointment” in Section 23 of the RTE Act encompasses not only initial appointment but also appointment by promotion, as recruitment broadly includes direct appointment, promotion, absorption, and transfer (¶¶192–196). The first proviso to Section 23(2), which requires all in-service teachers to acquire minimum qualifications (including the TET) within a prescribed period, was enacted specifically to extend the quality standards mandate beyond new appointees to those already in service; accepting the contention that the TET applies only to initial appointment would render this proviso nugatory (¶¶198–200). As a logical corollary, in-service teachers aspiring for promotion must qualify the TET irrespective of the length of their service (¶170). Mindful of the ground realities and of teachers who have spent decades in service prior to the RTE Act’s enactment, the Court invoked Article 142 and directed that teachers with less than five years of service remaining as on date may continue until superannuation without the TET but may not be considered for promotion without it (¶216). Teachers with more than five years remaining until superannuation must qualify the TET within two years, failing which they shall be compulsorily retired and paid terminal benefits to the extent of their qualifying service (¶217).Prepared by Sahil RatheeCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: K. UMADEVI vs THE GOVERNMENT OF TAMIL NADUC.A. No. 2526/2025
Subject: Entitlement to maternity leave for a child from a second marriage despite two children from a previous marriage
Judgemental Summary: K. UMADEVI V. THE GOVERNMENT OF TAMIL NADU2025 INSC 781 (23 May 2025)Justices:  Justice Abhay S Oka, Justice Ujjal BhuyanQuestion(s):  Whether a woman government servant, who has two biological children from a previous marriage who are not in her custody, is entitled to maternity leave for a child born from her second marriage.Factual Background:  The appellant entered government service in December 2012 as an English Teacher in a government higher secondary school in Tamil Nadu. Prior to her entry into service, she had married in 2006 and had two children, but that marriage was dissolved in 2017, and the children remained in the custody of her former husband. On 12 September 2018, the appellant remarried and subsequently applied for maternity leave from 17 August 2021 to 13 May 2022 due to a pregnancy from this second marriage.Respondent no. 3/ Chief Educational Officer rejected the application on 28 August 2021, citing Fundamental Rule (FR) 101(a), which restricts maternity leave to government employees in Tamil Nadu with less than two surviving children. A Single Judge of the High Court of Madras ("High Court") initially set aside this rejection, holding that "surviving children" should mean children in the lawful custody of the mother. However, a Division Bench of the High Court reversed this decision, ruling that maternity leave was not a fundamental right and the appellant was ineligible since she already had two children. The appellant subsequently assailed this decision before the Supreme Court.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the judgment of the Division Bench of the High Court, and declared that the appellant shall be granted maternity leave under Rule 101 (a) of the Tamil Nadu Fundamental Rules. Additionally, the Supreme Court directed that the maternity benefits admissible to the appellant shall be released to her within a period of two months from the date of the judgment. The judgement of the Court was authored by Justice Bhuyan.Reasons for the Decision:  Constitutional and International Framework The Supreme Court observed that Article 21 of the Constitution of India has been interpreted liberally to include the right to health and the right to live with human dignity (¶13, ¶13.1). The Court emphasized that Article 42 mandates the State to provide just and humane conditions of work and maternity relief, while Article 51(c) urges the fostering of respect for international law (¶14, ¶15). The Court further noted that international conventions like the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women ("CEDAW") recognize reproductive rights, including the right to decide freely on the number and spacing of children, as part of international human rights law (¶25.1, ¶25.2, ¶28).Reproductive Rights as a Facet of Article 21 The Supreme Court, relying on Suchita Srivastava v. Chandigarh Administration 2009 INSC 1086, observed that a woman’s right to make reproductive choices is a dimension of personal liberty under Article 21 (¶29). The Court also reiterated that the reproductive rights include an entitlement to carry a pregnancy to full term and raise children (¶29). Purposive Interpretation and Precedent The Supreme Court referred to the decision in Deepika Singh v. Central Administrative Tribunal 2022 INSC 834, where it was declared that the fact a spouse had children from a previous marriage would not impinge upon a woman's entitlement to maternity leave for her own biological child (¶33, ¶33.3). The Court reiterated that that provisions for maternity leave must be imbued with a purposive construction and a purpose-oriented approach to bridge the gap between law and society (¶33.2).Harmonisation of Policy Objectives The Supreme Court stated that while the policy to arrest population growth is a laudable objective, it must be harmonised with the objective of granting maternity benefits (¶35). The Court concluded that the two-child norm and the provision of maternity leave in these circumstances are not mutually exclusive and must be interpreted rationally to achieve social objectives (¶35).Prepared by Srishti BhartiCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: ALL INDIA JUDGES ASSOCIATION vs UNION OF INDIAW.P.(C) No. 1022/1989
Subject: Candidates desirous of appearing in Civil Judge (Junior Division) examination must have practiced for a minimum period of three years.
Judgemental Summary: ALL INDIA JUDGES ASSOCIATION V. UNION OF INDIA2025 INSC 735 (20 May 2025)Justices:  Chief Justice Bhushan R. Gavai, Justice Augustine G. Masih, Justice Krishnan V. ChandranQuestion(s):   (i) Should the 10% Limited Departmental Competitive Examination (“LDCE”) quota for promotion to District Judge be restored to 25%; and if so, should the minimum qualifying experience for this exam be reduced, and by how many years? (ii) Should a quota be reserved for meritorious Civil Judges (Junior Division) for promotion to Civil Judge (Senior Division) to incentivize merit within the cadre; and if so, what should be the percentage and minimum experience as a Civil Judge (Junior Division)? (iii) When calculating the quota for departmental examinations, should it be based on cadre strength or annual vacancies? (iv) Should a suitability test be introduced for promoting Civil Judges (Senior Division) to the District Judge cadre against the existing 65% quota? (v) Should the minimum practice requirement for appearing in the Civil Judge (Junior Division) examination be restored; and if so, how many years, and should this be calculated from provisional enrollment/registration or from passing the All India Bar Examination (“AIBE”)?Factual Background:  A writ petition was filed under Article 32 of the Constitution in the Supreme Court in 1989 by the working President of the All India Judges Association seeking  directions for setting up an All India Judicial Service and for bringing about uniform conditions of service for judges of the subordinate judiciary throughout the country. This writ resulted in the First All India Judges Association  case (1991 INSC 290) in which the Supreme Court directed the Union and state governments to take several steps to enhance the service conditions of the judges of the subordinate judiciary. Since then, several interlocutory applications have been filed in this case seeking the Supreme Court’s intervention to address several issues pertaining to the district  judiciary. After the judgment in the First All India Judges Association case, the Government of India constituted the First National Judicial Pay Commission under the Chairmanship of Justice K.J. Shetty (“Shetty Commission”). The Shetty Commission submitted its report on November 11, 1999. In the subsequent Third All India Judges Association case (2002 INSC 165), the Supreme Court considered these recommendations and discontinued the three-year practice requirement for the Civil Judge (Junior Division) Examination, which had been introduced by the Second All India Judges Association case (1993 INSC 272).Between 2019 to 2022, several applications were filed raising issues pertaining to the qualification, promotion and selection of candidates who were desirous of either entering the Judicial Services as Civil Judge (Junior Division) or Higher Judicial Service, and with regard to the promotions at different levels within the Judicial Services. The Supreme Court appointed  an amicus curiae and sought responses from the various High Courts and States of the country, to decide the above larger issues concerning the administration of justice.Decision of the Supreme Court:  A Three-Judge Bench of the Supreme Court restored the 25% quota for promotion of Civil Judge (Senior Division) to District Judge through the Limited Departmental Competitive Examination (LDCE), increasing it from the previous 10%. The Court also reduced the minimum qualifying experience for this promotion from five years to three years as a Civil Judge (Senior Division). The Court also fixed a quota of 10% for the meritorious Civil Judge (Junior Division) for the Promotion to Civil Judge (Senior Division) and also laid down certain parameters which the High Courts were directed to adopt while framing rules to determine the suitability test to promote Civil Judge (Senior Division) to District Judge cadre. The Court also restored the minimum practice requirement of three years  for appearing in the Civil Judge (Junior Division) examination which is to be counted from the date of provisional enrollment with the concerned Bar Council of the state. The judgment of the Court was authored by the Chief Justice Gavai.Reasons for the Decision:  Restoration of 10% LDCE Quota to 25% for Higher Judicial ServiceThe Supreme Court referred to the Shetty Commission's recommendation, which advocated for a 25% direct recruitment from advocates and 75% promotion for the District Judge cadre (¶15). The  Court found that in the Third All India Judges Association case it had broadly accepted these recommendations but had introduced a nuanced approach to promotions by mandating 50% by merit-cum-seniority (with a suitability test) and 25% strictly by merit through LDCE for Civil Judges (Senior Division) with a minimum of five years of qualifying service (¶16). The Court found that many High Courts faced significant challenges in filling the 25% LDCE quota, leading to numerous vacant posts. Therefore, the Court in the  Fourth All India Judges Association Case [(2010) 15 SCC 170] reduced the LDCE quota to 10% to address the large number of unfilled vacancies by finding it not good for judicial administration (¶18). The Court observed  that the reduction led to a modified recruitment ratio of 65% by regular promotion, 10% by LDCE, and 25% by direct recruitment from the Bar (¶19).The Supreme Court noted that some High Courts recommended restoring the LDCE quota to 25%, provided any unfilled seats could revert to regular promotion, while others preferred retaining the 10% quota (¶¶22-23). The Court held that restoring the LDCE quota to 25% would provide a crucial incentive for meritorious officers to achieve accelerated promotion (¶24). The Court reasoned that addressing the qualifying experience requirement would ensure a sufficient pool of eligible candidates for the LDCE (¶27). The Court further held that if, in a given year, sufficient candidates are not selected from the LDCE quota, those posts should revert to the regular promotion quota based on merit-cum-seniority in the same year, thus preventing any adverse impact on justice administration while simultaneously incentivizing meritorious judicial officers (¶28).Reduction of Minimum Qualifying Experience for LDCEThe Supreme Court referred to its Fifth All India Judges' Association Case (2022 INSC 445), where it had already considered and modified this requirement specifically for the Delhi Judicial Services, reducing the 5-year Civil Judge (Senior Division) experience (¶29). The Court found that various High Courts and State Governments held diverse opinions on reducing the 5-year experience requirement, with some opposing it and others recommending a reduction to three or two years (¶¶31-34). The Court observed that in many states, the average time for a Civil Judge (Junior Division) to reach the District Judge cadre through regular promotion was often similar to the time it took to become eligible for LDCE (¶¶39-40). The Court reasoned that this situation provided no actual incentive for a Judicial Officer to appear for LDCE (¶41). The Supreme Court held that to truly serve its purpose of enabling meritorious officers to gain accelerated promotion, it is desirable to modify the eligibility requirement for LDCE (¶42). The Court further held that the minimum number of years of experience as a Civil Judge (Senior Division) should be reduced from 5 years to 3 years. The Court further  held that the total cumulative experience for a Judicial Officer to be eligible for LDCE, including service as Civil Judge (Junior Division) and Civil Judge (Senior Division), should be a minimum of 7 years (¶42).Quota for Meritorious Civil Judges (Junior Division) to Civil Judge (Senior Division)The Supreme Court found that most State Governments already followed the practice of filling vacant seats based on the total cadre strength (¶47). Therefore, it held that a uniform practice should be followed by all states (¶48). The Court  reasoned that since most states already calculate cadre strength, maintaining uniformity would best be achieved by calculating the quota for LDCE on the basis of the cadre strength (¶48).Introduction of Suitability Test for Promotion to Higher Judicial Service (65% Merit-Cum-Seniority Quota)The Supreme Court referred to its Third All India Judges Association case where it had already held the necessity of judicial officers becoming more efficient and staying updated with legal developments (¶49). The Court found that its previous judgment had directed High Courts to devise and evolve a test in order to ascertain and examine the legal knowledge of those candidates and to assess their continued efficiency with adequate knowledge of case-law for the 50% merit-cum-seniority promotion quota (¶¶49-50). The Court found that some High Courts have already framed rules for determining suitability, taking into account factors such as the evaluation of judgments, Annual Confidential Reports (ACRs), disposal rates, pendency of disciplinary proceedings, and viva voce performance (¶51). The Court held that while no rigid formula can be laid down, High Courts that have not yet framed such rules must do so immediately (¶52). It also directed High Courts and State Governments to examine whether their existing rules are sufficient. The Supreme Court suggested that relevant factors for determining suitability should include whether the candidate possesses updated knowledge of law, the quality of judgments, ACRs of the preceding five years, disposal rates, viva voce performance, and general perceptions and communication skills (¶52).Minimum Practice Requirement for Appearing in Civil Judge (Junior Division) ExaminationThe Supreme  Court found that the  117th Law Commission Report's recommendations that  fresh law graduates should enter judicial service, provided they receive intensive training  was considered by it  in the the Second All India Judges Association case (1993 INSC 272), which  had previously mandated a three-year practice requirements (¶55). The Court held that the decision in the Second All India Judges Association case  stemmed from the recognition that recruiting "raw graduates" without prior lawyering experience had not been successful because judges, from their first day, handle critical matters of life, liberty, property, and reputation, making it imprudent to allow fresh graduates to assume such vital power (¶57). The Court had also held that neither book knowledge nor pre-service training could adequately substitute for the firsthand experience of court-system functioning and justice administration gained through legal practice (¶58).Revisiting the issue in the present case, the Supreme Court held  that the continued recruitment of fresh graduates had largely failed over the past two decades (¶¶81,83). The Court reasoned that practical exposure to courts, litigants, and their  briefs is crucial for developing sensitivity to human problems, fostering clarity in decision-making, and understanding the Bar's role in justice dispensation (¶82). It reiterated that theoretical knowledge and pre-service training cannot adequately substitute for direct experience within the court system (¶83). The Court held that affidavits from most High Courts revealed that fresh law graduates, lacking practice, faced difficulties with procedural issues, leading to decorum problems that impaired justice delivery (¶64, ¶67, ¶¶69-72). Therefore agreeing with the High Courts, the Court directed all High Courts and State Governments to amend their relevant service rules, making it mandatory for candidates appearing for the Civil Judge (Junior Division) examination to have a minimum of three years of legal practice to be eligible (¶¶84, 89). The Court also clarified that pursuant to their appointment as Civil Judge (Junior Division), the candidates must compulsorily undergo at least one year of training before presiding in a Court (¶89). Experience to be Counted from Provisional Registration Date with Bar CouncilThe Supreme Court also held  that  legal practice experience should commence from the date of provisional registration with the Bar Council and not after passing the All India Bar Examination (“AIBE”) because provisional registration permits candidates to practice within their respective states (¶¶85-86). The Court balanced  the necessity of practical experience with providing opportunities for meritorious young law graduates and held  that experience should be counted from the date of provisional registration (¶86).The Supreme Court also addressed the  concerns that candidates might obtain provisional registration solely to qualify for the Civil Judge (Junior Division) Examination without actual practice by introducing essential safeguards (¶87). The Court held that for Mofussil Courts, a certificate from the  Judicial Officers working  there confirming requisite practice of the concerned advocate  would be  mandatory (¶88). For larger stations or metropolitan cities, a certificate from an advocate with a minimum of 10 years standing, duly endorsed by a Principal Judicial Officer of that District was declared to  be mandatory (¶88). Similarly, for those practicing before High Courts or the Supreme Court, a comparable certificate from an advocate who has a minimum standing of  10 years  endorsed by a designated officer of that High Court or the Supreme Court, was to be required (¶88). The Court also held that experience gained as Law Clerks with any Judges or Judicial Officers across the country should be recognized when calculating a total three years of practice (¶88).Prepared by Raza Zaidi and Osama NoorCentre for Research and Planning© Supreme Court of IndiaLink to Judgment (PDF)

Title: VIJAYA BANK  vs PRASHANT B NARNAWAREC.A. No. 11708/2016
Subject: Validity of restrictive covenant in employment contract requiring minimum service period
Judgemental Summary: VIJAYA BANK V. PRASHANT B NARNAWARE2025 INSC 691 (14 May 2025)Justices:  Justice Pamidighantam S. Narasimha, Justice Joymalya BagchiQuestion(s):  (i) Whether Clause 11(k) of the appointment letter, which required a minimum service period and the payment of liquidated damages upon early resignation, amounted to a restraint of trade under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 (“ICA”).(ii) Whether the restrictive covenant was opposed to public policy under Section 23 of the ICA and violative of Articles 14 and 19 of the Constitution of India.Factual Background:  The respondent joined the appellant-bank as a Probationary Assistant Manager in 1999 and was later promoted to Middle Management Scale-II. In 2006, the appellant-bank issued a recruitment notification for the appointment of 349 officers, which stipulated that selected candidates must execute an indemnity bond of Rs. 2 lakhs to be paid if they left service before the completion of three years. The respondent applied for the post of Senior Manager-Cost Accountant and was selected. The appointment letter issued to him on 07 August 2007 contained Clause 11(k), which required him to serve for a minimum of three years and execute the aforementioned indemnity bond. The respondent accepted these conditions, joined the post on 28 September 2007, and executed the bond. On 17 July 2009, before completing three years, the respondent resigned to join another bank and subsequently paid the Rs. 2 lakhs under protest. He then filed a writ petition challenging the validity of the clause. The single judge allowed the writ petition and the order was also upheld by the Division Bench. The appellant-bank filed this appeal against that judgment. Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Division Bench of the Supreme Court allowed the appeal filed by the appellant-bank. The Court set aside the judgment and order of the High Court. The Court held that the restrictive covenant in Clause 11(k) of the appointment letter did not constitute a restraint of trade and was not opposed to public policy. The judgement of the Court was authored by Justice Bagchi.Reasons for the Decision:  Covenants during the Subsistence of EmploymentThe Supreme Court held that the validity of a restrictive covenant regarding the exercise of a lawful profession must be tested under Section 27 of the ICA (¶12). The Court distinguished between restrictive covenants that apply after the termination of a contract and those that operate during its subsistence (¶13). Relying on the case of Niranjan Shankar Golikari v. Century Spinning and Manufacturing Co 1967 INSC 10, the Court noted that negative covenants operative during the period of employment, where an employee is bound to serve an employer exclusively, are generally not regarded as a restraint of trade unless the contract is unconscionable, excessively harsh, or unreasonable (¶13). The Court found that Clause 11(k) sought to perpetuate the employment contract for a specified term in furtherance of the contract rather than restraining future employment (¶16).Public Policy and Administrative EfficiencyThe Court held that public policy is fundamentally rooted in the advancement of the public good and interest. Recognizing that standards of what is ‘just, fair, and reasonable’ are not static, the Court observed that these concepts evolve alongside societal progress. Consequently, civilizational growth, the expansion of knowledge, and evolving standards of human rights and dignity continually reshape the boundaries of public policy (¶24). The Court stated that in an era of liberalization, public sector undertakings like the appellant-bank must compete with private players, which requires ensuring the retention of efficient and experienced staff (¶26). The Court ruled that such a restrictive covenant was not unconscionable or unreasonable and thus not in contravention of public policy (¶27).Reasonableness of Liquidated DamagesThe Supreme Court accepted the appellant-bank’s contention that premature resignations cause financial hardship and administrative prejudice, as they render the elaborate recruitment process redundant and necessitate fresh, expensive competitive procedures (¶¶28, 29). The Court found that because the respondent served in a senior managerial grade with a lucrative pay package, the quantum of Rs. 2 lakhs in liquidated damages was not so high as to make the possibility of resignation illusory (¶31). The Court concluded that the High Court failed to consider the covenant in its proper factual matrix and had mechanically relied on inapplicable precedents (¶32).Prepared by Srishti BhartiCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: GAYATRI BALASAMY vs M/S ISG NOVASOFT TECHNOLOGIES LIMITEDC.A. No. 6178-6179/2025
Subject: Power of the Court to modify an Arbitral Award under Section 34 and 37 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.
Judgemental Summary: GAYATRI BALASAMY V. M/S ISG NOVASOFT TECHNOLOGIES LIMITED2025 INSC 605 (30 April 2025)Justices:  Chief Justice Sanjiv Khanna, Justice Bhushan R. Gavai, Justice Sanjay Kumar, Justice Kalpathy V. Viswanathan, Justice Augustine G. Masih.Question(s):  (i) Whether the Indian Courts are jurisdictionally empowered to modify an arbitral award. (ii) Whether the power to set aside an award under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996 being a larger power, will include the power to modify an arbitral award and if so, to what extent. (iii) Whether an arbitral award can be modified by using Article 142 of the Constitution.Factual Background:  Gayatri Balasamy (“Appellant”), an employee at ISG Novasoft Technologies Ltd., filed a criminal complaint against senior officers of the company for offences relating to sexual harassment which was met with criminal complaints of defamation and extortion by the company. When the matter reached the Supreme Court, it was referred to arbitration, where the Arbitral Tribunal awarded the Appellant  ?2 crores. Dissatisfied, she sought to set aside the award under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996 (“1996 Act”) before the Madras High Court. The High Court modified the award, granting an additional ?1.6 crores. However, a Division Bench on appeal under Section 37 of the 1996 Act reduced this additional award to ?50,000, deeming the initial compensation excessive. The Appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court.On February 20, 2024, a Three-Judge Bench of the Supreme Court identified an essential question of law regarding the power of courts to modify an arbitral award under Section 34 and 37 of the 1996 Act. The Court noted conflicting opinions in two lines of cases. Decisions like McDermot International Inc. v. Burn Standard Co. Ltd. and Others (2006 INSC 325) and Project Director, NHAI v. M Hakeem (2021 INSC 344) limited judicial intervention under Section 34 to express grounds, disallowing error correction, cost reconsideration, or merit review. Conversely, cases such as Vedanta Limited v. Shenzhen Shandong Nuclear Power Construction Company Ltd. (2018 INSC 959) and Oil and Natural Gas Corporation Ltd v. Western GECO International Ltd (2014 INSC 596) suggested setting aside arbitral awards or modification depending on the severability of the offending part. Consequently, the matter was referred to the Chief Justice of India, and a Constitution Bench (five judges) was constituted.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Supreme Court, by a 4:1 majority, held that courts possess a limited power under Section 34 and 37 of the 1996 Act to modify an arbitral award. The majority also affirmed the courts' power to modify post-award interest where justified. Chief Justice Khanna authored the majority judgment, while Justice Viswanathan wrote a dissenting opinion, holding that courts cannot modify an arbitral award.Reasons for the Decision:  Severability of Arbitral Awards under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996The majority held that the proviso to Section 34(2)(a)(iv) embodies the doctrine of severability, empowering courts to separate invalid portions of an arbitral award based on Section 34(2)(a) grounds while upholding valid parts (¶33 CJ. Khanna). This power of severability within the confines of Section 34 was deemed inherent in the Court’s jurisdiction (¶32 CJ. Khanna). Applying the doctrine of omne majus continent in se minus (a greater power includes the lesser power) to the proviso of Section 32(2)(a)(iv), the majority held that the power to set aside an award necessarily includes the power to partially set it aside (¶34 CJ. Khanna). The majority held that this partial setting aside, was only permissible when valid and invalid portions were clearly segregable (¶36 CJ. Khanna).Justice Viswanathan, in his dissenting opinion, largely agreed that Section 34 inherently recognizes the power to sever parts of an arbitral award. However, he found that there was a conceptual difference between a “modification” and “severance” (¶8). He reasoned that Section 34(2)(a)(iv) and its proviso explicitly permit setting aside only the part of an award dealing with disputes not contemplated by or beyond the scope of arbitration, provided such decisions are severable (¶¶143-45). He found  this concept of severability extends to setting aside standalone claims that violate Section 34, provided they are separable and not intertwined (¶146, ¶149). He further held that the power to set aside includes partial setting aside and severing offending portions, provided the annulled part is independent and its removal does not impact other findings (¶152, ¶153). He held  that if a compromise affects only a severable portion, a Section 34 court can give effect to it by severing that part (¶154, ¶155). However, if the compromised aspects are inseparably intertwined, the court must either set aside the award or dismiss the Section 34 application (¶155).Justice Viswanathan, however, held that the power to modify an arbitral award is not a lesser power subsumed within the power to set aside. He reasoned that the legal maxim omne majus continet in se minus does not apply to Section 34 of the 1996 Act because the qualitative nature of appellate power under the  Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 fundamentally differs from Section 34 power, operating in distinct spheres (¶¶84-88, ¶91). He reasoned  that modifying an award, which involves a court adjudicating on merits after parties agreed to arbitration, cannot be subsumed within the power to set aside (¶93). He found it untenable for the Court to read in the power to modify, given Parliament's repeated omissions of such a provision (¶95).A limited power of modification is located within the meaning of Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996The majority found that the 1996 Act aimed to expedite dispute resolution through a quicker, cost-effective alternative to litigation (¶40 CJ. Khanna). It held that denying courts the power to modify would lead to absurd outcomes,  impose hardships, increase costs, and cause delays, as Section 34 and 37 appeals often take years (¶41 CJ. Khanna). The majority reasoned that if courts could only set aside awards, parties would endure an extra round of arbitration to affirm a decision easily reached by the court (¶42 CJ. Khanna). Thus, the majority held that the power of judicial review under Section 34 and the setting aside of an arbitral award inherently include a limited power to modify the arbitral award (¶46 CJ. Khanna).Justice Viswanathan  held that courts lack the power to modify arbitral awards under Section 34 of the 1996 Act. He reasoned  that the 1996 Act’s focus on minimal judicial intervention, the absence of an express modification power (unlike the 1940 Act), and the plain language of Section 34—which only permits setting aside—establish this limitation (¶¶59,70,76). He rejected "reading in" the power to modify, deeming it unwarranted judicial overreach that contradicts clear legislative intent (¶90).Justice Viswanathan further  held that setting aside an award does not lead to an absurd outcome, as the 1996 Act allows for recommencement of proceedings, including arbitration, upon setting aside an award under Section 43(4). He reasoned that parties entering arbitration consciously agree to step out of the normal judicial process, and a Section 34 court, unless expressly authorized, cannot modify or vary an award (¶77, ¶81).Power of modification is different from remandThe majority found that under Section 34(4) of the 1996 Act, courts are empowered to remit issues back to the arbitral tribunal for specific determinations (¶56 CJ. Khanna). Upon remand, the tribunal can vary, correct, review, add, or modify the award. However, the majority held that Section 34(4) does not authorize the tribunal to rewrite or set aside the award on merits; rather, it acts as a curative mechanism available when permitted by the court (¶60 CJ. Khanna). The majority  held that computational, clerical, and typographical errors not requiring a merits evaluation can be modified by the Court in a Section 34 application (¶53 CJ. Khanna). The Supreme Court discarded the view in Kinnari Mullick v. Ghanshyam Das Damani (2017 INSC 1281), which required only a written application for remand under Section 34(4), holding that under Section 37 of the 1996 Act, the Court still possesses jurisdiction to remand the matter suo motu (¶62 CJ. Khanna, ¶¶131-132 J. Vishwanathan).Justice Viswanathan found that Section 33 allows the Arbitral Tribunal to correct computational, clerical, or similar errors and make additional awards for unadjudicated claims (¶¶128-129). He held that the power to adjourn the proceedings and remit the matter to the Arbitral Tribunal under Section 34(4) can be exercised for undoing the curable defects(¶134). He found that this power is to cure defects like inadequate reasoning or filling gaps in existing findings, but not for supplying findings on contentious issues absent from the original award (¶134, ¶¶141-142). He held that a limited exception  based on the actus curiae neminem gravabit principle allows the court under Section 34 to correct obvious computational, clerical, or typographical errors without modifying the original award's terms even if the arbitrator has not exercised its power under Section 33 (¶137, ¶¶140-141).Modification does not render the amended award unenforceable under the New York ConventionThe majority held that interpreting Section 34 to include a limited power to modify awards will not affect the enforcement of foreign awards under international commercial arbitration (¶66 CJ. Khanna). The majority reasoned that Article V of the New York Convention and Section 46 of the 1996 Act, similarly worded, recognize the supremacy of the domestic law of the country where the award is made for enforcement purposes (¶67 CJ. Khanna). However, Justice Viswanathan held that modifying New York Convention awards in India would complicate foreign enforcement due to the lack of statutory provisions (unlike UK/Singapore) that treat a modified award as part of the Tribunal's award (¶121, ¶123). He pointed out that this issue warrants legislative intervention (¶ J. Viswanathan ¶123).Court can modify post award interest but not pendente liteThe majority held that the Court cannot modify pendente lite interest granted during arbitration (¶72 CJ. Khanna). Where the interest awarded contradicts the contractual position, the court, when examining objections under Section 34, can either set aside the interest rate or remand the matter to the arbitral tribunal under Section 34(4) (¶73 CJ. Khanna). The majority observed that post-award interest under Section 37(1)(b) is an additional compensation for unpaid arbitral awards after due time (¶74 CJ. Khanna). In cases where this post-award interest appears unjustified, given the unpredictability of the future unknown to the arbitrator, it is necessary for the Court to have the authority to modify it for the dissipation of justice (¶76 CJ. Khanna).Justice Vishwanthan in his dissenting opinion held that a court cannot modify interest, including post-award interest, and the proper course is to remit the matter to the arbitrator for correction under Section 34(4) (¶135, ¶156). He also held that the Court may suo motu exercise this Section 34(4) power if it finds grounds to set aside the award, as its purpose is to allow the tribunal to cure defects and make the award enforceable (¶136). Supreme Court’s power to do complete justice under Article 142 of the ConstitutionThe majority  held that the Supreme Court's power under Article 142 of the Constitution must be exercised in consonance with the fundamental principles and objectives of the 1996 Act (¶82 CJ. Khanna). The majority reasoned that this power can be used in arbitration proceedings to end protracted litigation and save parties' time and money.  However, it should not be used to rewrite or modify the award on merits (¶84 CJ. Khanna).Justice Viswanathan, in his dissenting opinion, held that the Supreme Court's powers under Article 142 of the Constitution cannot be exercised to modify an arbitral award when matters arise from Section 34 of the 1996 Act. He reasoned that established jurisprudence on Article 142 clearly states it cannot be used to "supplant" substantive law, achieve indirectly what cannot be achieved directly, or contradict express statutory provisions. He held that modifying an arbitral award under Article 142 strikes at the core ethos of the arbitration process, breaching a pre-eminent prohibition within the 1996 Act. He found that such intervention would create grave uncertainties for contracting parties and be antithetical to arbitration as an effective dispute resolution mode (¶¶ 108-115).Prepared by Raza Zaidi and Osama NoorCentre for Research and Planning | Supreme Court of IndiaCase Materials:  Day 1 of Arguments: 13 February 2025 (Argument Transcript ) | (Video Recording)  Day 2 of Arguments: 18 February 2025 (Argument Transcript) | (Video Recording) Day 3 of Arguments: 19 February 2025 (Argument Transcripts) | (Video Recording)

Title: RUTU MIHIR PANCHAL vs UNION OF INDIAW.P.(C) No. 282/2021
Subject: Constitutionality of determining pecuniary jurisdiction of consumer commissions under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019 on the basis of consideration paid instead of compensation claimed
Judgemental Summary: RUTU MIHIR PANCHAL V. UNION OF INDIA2025 INSC 593 (29 April 2025)Justices:  Justice Pamidighantam S. Narasimha, Justice Manoj MisraQuestion(s):  (i) Whether Sections 34(1), 47(1)(a)(i) and 58(1)(a)(i) of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, which prescribe pecuniary jurisdictions on the basis of value of goods and services paid as consideration instead of compensation claimed, are constitutionally valid?(ii) Whether empowering the district, state and national commissions to exercise jurisdiction on the basis of value of the goods or services paid as consideration is violative of Article 14?Factual Background:  The proceedings arose from two separate matters: a writ petition and a civil appeal. In the writ petition, the petitioner’s husband purchased a Ford Endeavour Titanium car for Rs. 31.19 Lakhs. The vehicle caught fire while being driven, leading to his death. The petitioner initiated consumer proceedings for Rs. 51.49 crores in compensation before the District Commission. She alleged that the 2019 Act compelled her to approach the District Commission based on the purchase price, whereas under the repealed 1986 Act, she could have approached the National Commission directly based on the compensation claimed.In the civil appeal, the appellant's husband passed away due to COVID-19, and she sought Rs. 14.94 crore in compensation based on an insurance policy. The National Commission rejected her petition on the ground that the consideration paid for the insurance policy did not exceed Rs. 10 crores. The petitioner/ appellant challenged the constitutional validity of Sections 34, 47 and 58 of the 2019 Act, which shifts the basis of the pecuniary jurisdiction of the District, State, and National Commissions from the value of compensation claimed under the repealed Consumer Protection Act, 1986 to the value of the consideration paid for the goods or services.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Supreme Court dismissed the constitutional challenge and declared that Sections 34, 47, and 58 of the 2019 Act are constitutional. The Court held that the provisions are neither violative of Article 14 nor manifestly arbitrary. Furthermore, the Court directed the Central Consumer Protection Council and the Central Consumer Protection Authority to discharge their statutory duties by surveying and reviewing the functioning of the statute and advising the government on effective redressal measures. The judgment was authored by Justice Narasimha.Reasons for the Decision:  I. Legislative Competence to Prescribe Pecuniary LimitsThe Supreme Court held that the Parliament has the legislative competence to prescribe the jurisdiction and powers of a court, which includes the power to prescribe pecuniary limits (¶9.1). Citing State of Bombay v. Narottamdas Jethabhai (1950 INSC 38), the Court observed that this power forms an essential aspect to the constitution and organisation of all courts and is a practice of widest amplitude (¶9.1). The Court also noted that the parliament has enacted the Consumer Protection Act under Entry 95 of List I read with Entries 11-A and 46 of List III and in exercise of power under Article 246 (¶9).II. Validity of Classification under Article 14 The Supreme Court held that the classification based on value of goods or services on the basis of the amount paid as consideration is valid (¶10.2). Noting consideration as an integral part of forming a contract, the Court observed that whether paid in the present, future, or through deferred payment, the existence of such consideration is the essential requirement to be classified as a consumer under the Act (¶¶10.2,10.4). The court also held that since consideration is the essential core of a transaction, using it to determine pecuniary jurisdiction is neither illegal nor discriminatory (¶¶10.3, 10.5).III. Rational Nexus to Statutory Objects The Supreme Court reasoned that the classification established by the 2019 Act has a direct nexus to the object sought to be achieved and does not constitute a suspect classification. The Court also noted that the value of consideration paid for a good or service is closer and more easily relatable to compensation than a consumer's self-assessed claim for damages. Consequently, the Court clarified that determining the jurisdiction of the various commissions on the basis of the consideration paid bears a rational nexus to the objective of providing a hierarchy of judicial remedies. (¶11).     IV. No Loss of Judicial Remedy The Supreme Court clarified that no judicial remedy is lost, as the relief or compensation a consumer may claim remains unrestricted irrespective of the forum. It observed that it is well settled that a consumer has no right or privilege to choose a forum of his choice by raising an unlimited claim. The Court further noted that a court or tribunal always retains the jurisdiction to prevent abuse of the process of law by scrutinising claims that are grossly overvalued or undervalued (¶11.1).V. Constitutional Duty of Performance Audit The Supreme Court emphasized that reviewing and assessing the implementation of a statute is an integral part of the Rule of Law (¶12.3). It observed that the Executive has a constitutional duty to monitor the functioning of a statute and undertake a continuous, real-time assessment of its impact (¶12.4). The Court further recognized that the vibrant functioning of the statutory Council and Authority would advance the objectives of the 2019 legislation in safeguarding consumer interests (¶12.17).Prepared by Jemima B SCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: VARSHATAI vs THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRAC.A. No. 5187-5188/2025
Subject: Validity of using Urdu as an additional language for official purposes in Maharashtra.
Judgemental Summary: VARSHATAI V. THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA2025 INSC 486 (15 April 2025)Justices:  Justice Sudhanshu Dhulia and Justice Krishnan V. Chandran Question(s):  (i) Whether the High Court of Bombay was correct in concluding that the Maharashtra Local Authorities (Official Languages) Act, 2022 does not prohibit the use of Urdu as an additional language on the signboard of a Municipal Council building in Maharashtra.Factual Background:  A signboard of the new building of the Municipal Council, Patur in district Akola, Maharashtra displayed the text “Municipal Council, Patur”, in Marathi at the top followed by its Urdu translation. The appellant objected to the use of Urdu, contending that since Marathi was the official language of the State of Maharashtra, the work of the Municipal Council could only be conducted in Marathi and therefore the use of Urdu in any manner was impermissible. The Municipal Council rejected these objections. Subsequently, the appellant moved an application under Section 308 of the Maharashtra Township Act, 1965 before the Collector Akola, praying for setting aside of the Municipal Council’s resolution which was allowed. The Municipal Council challenged the Collector’s actions before the Divisional Commissioner, Amravati and the Collector’s order was set aside. Aggrieved, the appellant filed a Writ Petition before the Bombay High Court seeking prohibition on the use of Urdu in all the work conducted by government and government bodies in Maharashtra. However, the High Court did not consider it a case calling for interference. Aggrieved, the appellant challenged the High Court’s order before the Supreme Court. During the pendency of the appeal, the Maharashtra Local Authorities (Official Languages) Act, 2022 (“2022 Act”) had been enacted. The appellant contended that under Section 3 of the 2022 Act, the use of Urdu on the signboard of the Municipal Council could not be permitted. The Supreme Court, observed that the 2022 Act had come into force during the pendency of appeal and therefore the appellant should again approach the High Court under the changed legislative position. The High Court heard the matter again and decided that even under the new legislation i.e 2022 Act, the use of an additional language to display the name of Municipal Council on its building would not indicate any violation. Thus, the appellant approached the Supreme Court in the present proceedings. Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Division Bench (two judges) of the Supreme Court upheld the decision of the High Court holding that there was no prohibition on the use of Urdu under the 2022 Act. The judgment of the Court was authored by Justice Dhulia. Reasons for the Decision:  Language essentially is a tool for effective communication The Division Bench (two judges) observed that Section 3(2) of the 2022 Act provides for the use of English in situations where communication cannot be properly conveyed in Marathi or in situations where the persons to whom such communications are addressed cannot understand Marathi. This indicates that the 2022 Act recognizes that language is essentially a tool for effective communication (¶15). The Supreme Court further held that the primary aim of the establishment of a Municipal Council is to cater to the immediate needs of the local community of its respective area. If the members of the local community in Patur are familiar with Urdu then there should not be any objection if Urdu is used in addition to the official language on the signboard of the Municipal Council’s building since language is a medium for the exchange of ideas that bring people holding diverse views and beliefs closer and it should not become a cause of their division (¶46).   Language is representative of a cultureThe Division Bench (two judges) observed that with more than 122 major languages including the 22 scheduled languages mentioned in the Constitution, hundreds of mother tongues and thousands of dialects used throughout the country, India is the most multilingual country in the world. However, this linguistic diversity makes the discussion on language both sensitive and delicate since each of these languages is representative of a respective culture, community or region (¶21). The Supreme Court further observed that Urdu is a representative of the composite cultural ethos or ganga-jamuni tehzeeb of the plains of northern and central India which has developed over the course of centuries (¶18). During the independence movement, when a need was felt for the greater use of the Indian languages, ‘Hindustani’ which is an amalgamation of various Indian languages especially Urdu and Hindi, was given popular support for the purpose of national integration (¶28). The Division Bench (two judges) further underscored the importance of adhering to the constitutional value of ‘tolerance’ while dealing with the linguistic diversity in India (¶21). Moreover, Article 351 of the Constitution emphasizes on the spread of Hindi language by assimilating the forms and expressions used in Hindustani and other languages of the Eighth Schedule of the Constitution imbibes the idea of constitutional tolerance while dealing with the issue of linguistic diversity in India (¶22).Considering practical necessity, States can permit the use of more than one languages for official purposesThe Division Bench (two judges) observed that Article 345 of the Constitution empowers the State legislature to adopt Hindi or any other language in use in that State as the official language of the State (¶23). However, a Constitution Bench (five judges) of the Supreme Court in Uttar Pradesh Hindi Sahitya Sammelan v. State of Uttar Pradesh (2014 INSC 594) while dealing with the issue of adoption of Urdu as the second official language by the State of Uttar Pradesh had held that mere adoption of a particular language as the official language of the State does not bar the State legislature from again invoking powers under Article 345 to designate yet another language as the official language(s) if required (¶25). The Supreme Court further observed that various State and Union Territories in India have more than one official language, or permit the use of more than one language for official purposes based upon the practical necessity to ensure effective communication with the people therein (¶26). Urdu not alien to India rather it was born and flourished IndiaThe Division Bench (two judges) observed that Urdu is an Indo-Aryan language just like Marathi and Hindi etc. It developed and flourished in India due to the need of the people belonging to different cultures who wanted to exchange ideas and communicate among themselves leading its development as a composite language.(¶27). The Supreme Court underscored that even today the common people in India use Urdu words in their everyday life to an extent that it will not be possible to have a day to day conversation without them (¶37). In fact, the States such as Andhra Pradesh, Bihar, Jharkhand, Telangana, Uttar Pradesh, and West Bengal, while the Union Territories of  Delhi and Jammu and Kashmir have adopted Urdu as one of their official languages (¶39).The Supreme Court noted that the Indian Courts have a heavy influence of Urdu in court jargons/parlance both in civil and criminal law. From adalat, halafnama, vakalatnama to peshi and dasti, this influence is writ large in the language of the Indian Courts (¶38). Therefore the Supreme Court emphasized that it was important to celebrate the linguistic diversity of India by making friends with Urdu and every other language (¶48). Prepared by Raza ZaidiCentre for Research and Planning |  Supreme Court of India © Supreme Court of India

Title: CRYOGAS EQUIPMENT PRIVATE LIMITED vs INOX INDIA LIMITEDC.A. No. 5174/2025
Subject: Two-pronged approach for classification under section 15(2) of the Copyright Act, 1957
Judgemental Summary: CRYOGAS EQUIPMENT PRIVATE LIMITED V. INOX INDIA LIMITED2025 INSC 483 (15 April 2025)Justices:  Justice Surya Kant, Justice Nongmeukapam K. Singh Question(s):  (i) What are the parameters for determining whether a work or an article falls within the limitation set out in Section 15(2) of the Copyright Act, 1957, thereby classifying it as a design under Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, 2000?(ii) Whether the High Court erred in setting aside the order of the Commercial Court and thus rejecting the application under Order VII Rule 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure (CPC)?Factual Background:  The Respondent, Inox India Limited (Inox), filed a suit against the Appellants (Cryogas and LNG Express), alleging they had infringed two categories of copyright: (i) Proprietary Engineering Drawings of LNG Semi-trailers and (ii) Literary Works consisting of details, processes and descriptions created by Inox employees. Inox sought a declaration, permanent injunction and damages, asserting these IPs were developed to meet specific requirements for Indian roads.In response, the LNG Express filed an application under Order VII Rule 11 of the CPC, seeking the rejection of the suit. They contended that the Proprietary Engineering Drawings fell under the definition of a design under Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, and that Inox had lost its copyright protection by failing to register them under the Designs Act. They specifically argued that under Section 15(2) of the Copyright Act, copyright ceases once a design is reproduced more than fifty times by an industrial process. The Appellants claimed Inox’s reported revenue of Rs. 122 crores suggested the production of more than fifty trailers, thus triggering the statutory bar.The Commercial Court initially allowed the Appellants' application and rejected the plaint. However, the High Court of Gujarat intervened, setting aside the rejection and remanding the matter. When the Commercial Court again rejected the plaint upon reconsideration, the High Court once more set aside the order. Consequently, the High Court restored the suit and directed the Commercial Court to decide on the interim injunction applications on their merits.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Supreme Court dismissed the appeals and upheld the High Court’s decision to reject the application under Order VII Rule 11, holding that whether the Proprietary Engineering Drawings qualify as artistic works or designs involves a significant mixed question of law and fact that cannot be decided at a preliminary stage. The Court further directed the Commercial Court consider the issue afresh and conduct trial by applying the test laid by the court to ascertain the true nature of the Proprietary Engineering Drawings and the other related IP right infringements claimed by Inox and to decide the pending interim injunction within two months. The judgment was authored by Justice Surya Kant.Reasons for the Decision:  I.
