Distinction Between Artistic Work and DesignThe Supreme Court reasoned that while artistic work under Section 2(c) of the Copyright Act has a broad connotation including abstract work(s) comprising a few arbitrarily drawn lines or curves in two or three dimensions, design under Section 2(d) of the Designs Act is restricted to specific features like shape, configuration, or pattern applied to an article by employing an industrial process which may be manual, mechanical or chemical (¶¶55,56,58). The court further observed that the legislative intent is to harmonise the two Statutes so that while an ‘artistic work’ qualifies for copyright protection, its commercial or industrial application i.e., the ‘design’ derived from the original work for industrial production is subject to the limitations set out in Section 15(2) of the Copyright Act (¶57).II. The Aspect of Functional Utility The Supreme Court observed that courts apply the test of “functional utility” to determine whether a work qualifies for protection under the Designs Act (¶39, 59). This requires an assessment as to whether the article serves a functional purpose beyond mere artistic expression; if the primary characteristic of the work is its functional utility rather than aesthetic appeal, it would not qualify (¶20). The Court further relied on decisions of English courts to hold that it must be determined whether the features of an article exist solely to make the article work or whether they also appeal to the eye  (¶32,33). III. The Two-Pronged Approach to Section 15(2) of the Copyright ActThe Supreme Court formulated a two-pronged approach to open the conundrum caused by Section 15(2) of the Copyright Act so as to ascertain whether a work is qualified to be protected by the Designs Act(¶60). The test considers:(i) whether the work in question is purely an artistic work entitled to protection under the Copyright Act or whether it is a design derived from such original artistic work and subjected to an industrial process? (¶60) (ii) If the work does not qualify for copyright protection, the test of functional utility will have to be applied so as to determine its dominant purpose and ascertain whether it qualifies for design protection (¶60)The court also emphasized that the courts applying this test, ought to undertake a case specific inquiry guided by statutory provisions, judicial precedents, and comparative jurisprudence (¶61).IV. Improper Use of Order VII Rule 11 The Supreme Court found that the Commercial Court had misread the plaint and misapplied legal principles (¶68). At the stage of Order VII Rule 11, the court is only required to conduct a prima facie inquiry to see if a cause of action is disclosed (¶¶66-67). The Court agreed with the High Court that the question pertaining to ascertaining the true nature of the ‘Proprietary Engineering Drawings’ involves a mixed question of law and fact (¶67).Prepared by Jemima B SCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: IMRAN PRATAPGADHI vs STATE OF GUJARATCrl.A. No. 1545/2025
Subject: Posting a poem promoting sacrifice and non-violence does not attract offences under Sections 196, 197, 299, 302, or 57 BNS; FIR registration in such cases is abuse of law.
Judgemental Summary: IMRAN PRATAPGADHI V. STATE OF GUJARAT2025 INSC 410 (Invalid Date)Justices:  Justice Abhay S. Oka, Justice Ujjal BhuyanQuestion(s):   Whether the recitation and posting of a poem by the appellant constituted offences punishable under Sections 196, 197, 299, 302, and 57 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (BNS), and whether registration of the FIR violated the appellant’s fundamental rights under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution.Factual Background:  The appellant, a Member of the Rajya Sabha, had posted on his verified ‘X’ (formerly Twitter) account a video clip from a mass marriage ceremony at which a poem was recited. The poem, in Urdu, metaphorically spoke of facing injustice with love and sacrificing personal losses for truth. A complaint alleged that the poem incited enmity between communities, promoted hatred, and harmed national unity. FIR was registered under Sections 196, 197(1), 302, 299, 57, and 3(5) of the BNS. The High Court dismissed the appellant’s petition seeking quashing of the FIR. The appellant approached the Supreme CourtDecision of the Supreme Court:   The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, quashed the FIR, and held that registration of the FIR in respect of the posted poem was a mechanical exercise without application of mind, amounted to a clear abuse of process of law, and violated the fundamental right of the appellant under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution.Reasons for the Decision:  Meaning of the Poem and Lack of OffenceThe Court, after carefully analysing the Urdu text and its English translation (¶9), concluded that the poem “has nothing to do with any religion, community, region or race” (¶10(a)). It neither affects national integration nor jeopardizes sovereignty, unity, or integrity of India (¶10(b), (c)). The Court emphasized that the poem “preaches non-violence” and encourages meeting injustice with love and sacrifice, not by resorting to violence (¶10(f), ¶10(h)). It noted that the reference to the "throne" was symbolic and denoted resistance against injustice rather than incitement against any community (¶10(g)).No Ingredients of Offences Made Out The Court exhaustively examined the ingredients of Section 196 of the BNS (¶13-14) and observed that the poem did not promote disharmony, hatred, or ill-will between religious, racial, linguistic or regional groups. The poem neither disturbed public tranquility nor sought to create disharmony (¶14). The offence under Section 197 was also ruled out as there was no assertion or imputation against any religious, racial, language, regional group, caste, or community (¶16). The Court found the invocation of Section 299 “ridiculous” (¶17), as the poem did not outrage religious feelings or insult religious beliefs. Similarly, the allegation under Section 302, requiring deliberate intention to wound religious feelings, was found wholly baseless (¶18). Section 57 concerning abetment by the public was found to have no nexus, as there was no act of abetment proved or alleged (¶19).Duty to Respect Freedom of Speech and Role of Preliminary Inquiry under BNSS The Court emphasized the mandatory requirement of registration of FIR under Section 173(1) of BNSS only if a cognizable offence is disclosed (¶20-22). However, the Court clarified that sub-section (3) of Section 173 allows a preliminary inquiry even where the offence is cognizable if the offence is punishable for three years or more but less than seven years (¶23-24). In the present case, since the offences alleged fell within that range (except Section 57), the police ought to have conducted a preliminary inquiry (¶28-29).The Court stressed that where allegations concern spoken or written words potentially attracting the exception under Article 19(2) of the Constitution, the police must exercise particular care and undertake preliminary inquiry (¶30). The obligation of the police to respect constitutional ideals under Article 51-A(a) was highlighted (¶29).Standard to Judge Alleged Offensive Speech Quoting the paragraph 67 of the judgment authored by Bose and Puranik, JJ in Bhagwati Charan Shukla v. Provincial Government, C.P. & Berar (1946 SCC OnLine MP 5), the Court held that the effect of words must be judged “using the standards of reasonable, strong-minded, firm and courageous men, and not those of weak and vacillating minds” (¶32-33). The Court reaffirmed that the poem, viewed from this standard, did not incite hatred or enmity and in fact promoted resilience against injustice through peaceful means.Requirement of Mens Rea The Court reiterated that mens rea is an essential ingredient for offences under Section 196, following its earlier decisions in Manzar Sayeed Khan v. State of Maharashtra (2007) 5 SCC 1 and Patricia Mukhim v. State of Meghalaya (2021) 15 SCC 35 (¶34). It held that there was no deliberate intention on part of the appellant to promote enmity or ill-will, and thus, even on prima facie basis, the necessary mens rea was absent. The Court severely criticized the High Court for failing to appreciate the fundamental constitutional protection of freedom of speech (¶35-37). It noted that the High Court wrongly relied on the "nascent stage of investigation" to refuse quashing the FIR despite no prima facie case being made out. It reaffirmed that Courts can and must quash proceedings at the initial stage itself to prevent abuse of process (¶37).Importance of Protecting Freedom of Expression The Court eloquently reaffirmed that free expression is "an integral part of a healthy, civilised society" and is indispensable to a dignified life under Article 21 (¶38). Courts must be vigilant to protect constitutional rights even if the content of speech is unpopular or uncomfortable to some (¶39-40). Reliance was placed on Anand Chintamani Dighe v. State of Maharashtra (2001 SCC OnLine Bom 891) and Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) 5 SCC 1 to reiterate that tolerance for dissent and non-mainstream views is a cardinal constitutional value (¶40-41). The Court summarized in ¶42-44 that Section 173(3) of BNSS permits preliminary inquiry even where a cognizable offence is disclosed if the punishment is between three to seven years. The police must act sensitively in such matters, particularly where free speech under Article 19(1)(a) is concerned. The Court emphasized that registration of FIRs based on speech must not stifle fundamental freedoms unless reasonable restrictions under Article 19(2) are genuinely attracted.Prepared by Iram JanCommunications Division|  Supreme Court of India© Supreme Court of India

Title: AMIT KUMAR vs UNION OF INDIACrl.A. No. 1425/2025
Subject: Constitution of National Task Force to address the mental health concerns of students and prevent the commission of suicides in Higher Educational Institutions
Judgemental Summary: AMIT KUMAR V. UNION OF INDIA2025 INSC 384 (24 March 2025)Justices:  Justice Jamshed B. Pardiwala, Justice R. MahadevanQuestion(s):  (i) Whether recourse to inquest proceedings under Section 174 of the CrPC obviates the requirement of registration of F.I.R.?Factual Background:  The case involves two young boys, Ayush Ashna and Anil Kumar, who were found dead in their respective hostel rooms at the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT), Delhi, in July and September 2023. Ayush Ashna was found dead on 08 July 2023, in Udaigiri Hostel, and Anil Kumar was found dead on 01 September 2023, in Vindhyanchal Hostel. Both students belonged to Scheduled Castes. The appellants, parents of the deceased students, alleged foul play and commission of offence under the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989.The Police did not register a First Information Report (F.I.R.) but instead conducted an inquiry under Section 174 of the CrPC. At the conclusion of this inquiry, the Police reached the determination that both students had committed suicide due to depression and an inability to cope with the pressure of their studies. The Appellants approached the High Court of Delhi seeking a writ of mandamus for the registration of an F.I.R. and an independent inquiry, but the High Court  declined to entertain the writ petition.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, ruling that inquest proceedings under Section 174 are limited in scope to the apparent cause of death and cannot be used as a substitute for a full investigation into cognizable offences. The Court directed the DCP (South-West District, New Delhi) to register the First Information Report in line with the two respective complaints and depute an officer not below the rank of Assistant Commissioner of Police to undertake the investigation. Furthermore, the Court constituted a National Task Force, chaired by Justice S. Ravindra Bhat, former Judge of the Supreme Court of India, to address mental health concerns among students and to prevent suicides in higher educational institutions. Additionally, the Task Force was requested to present an interim report within four months.The Court alsodirected the Union of India to deposit an amount of Rupees Twenty Lacs (Rs 20,00,000/-) with the Registry within two weeks as an outlay for the initial operations of the Task Force.Reasons for the Decision:  I. Mandatory Registration of F.I.R. under Section 154Following the settled law in Lalita Kumari v. Government of U.P. (2013 INSC 748), the Supreme Court held that when an informant approaches the police with information regarding the commission of a cognizable offence, the police owe a duty to promptly register an F.I.R. The court also observed that the police authorities are not vested with any discretion to conduct a preliminary inquiry to assess the credibility of the information before registering the F.I.R.(¶¶33-34). Citing State of Haryana v. Bhajan Lal (1990 INSC 363), the Court emphasized that reasonableness and credibility are not conditions precedent for registration (¶32).II. Distinction between Section 174 and Section 154 of the CrPCThe Supreme Court clarified that proceedings under Section 174 are limited in scope and confined to the ascertainment of the apparent cause of death, whereas the investigation undertaken after the registration of an F.I.R. under Section 154 constitutes an investigation into an offence (¶¶22, 27). The Court observed that inquest proceedings are not to find out who are the offenders and should not be equated with investigation into cognizable offences under Sections 160 and 161 of the CrPC respectively (¶¶ 22, 28).III. Mandate of the SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989The Supreme Court highlighted that under Section 18-A of the SC/ST (PoA) Act, no preliminary enquiry is required for the registration of an F.I.R. (¶¶ 42-43). It observed that this statutory mandate leaves no room for authorities to delve into an investigation to determine the reasons for suicide when categorical statements regarding caste-based harassment have been made in the complaints (¶43).IV. Doctrine of Loco Parentis and Institutional ResponsibilityThe Supreme Court held that the responsibility of maintaining the safety and well-being of students rests heavily on the administration of educational institutions (¶47). The Court observed that, under the principle of loco parentis, when a student is sent to a school or college, the authorities are obligated tosafeguard the student’s interests and welfare (¶66). The Court further emphasised that this duty extends beyond ensuring academic excellence to safeguarding students’ mental well-being by providing support in times of distress (¶66). The Court also stated that, in the event of an unfortunate incident like suicide, the institution bears an unequivocal duty to promptly lodge an F.I.R. (¶47).V. Necessity of a National Task ForceNoting a disturbing pattern of student suicides across the country linked to ragging, academic pressure, caste-based discrimination,and sexual harassment, the Supreme Court deemed it necessary to go beyond a mere direction to register F.I.R.s (¶¶49,54). It accordingly constituted a National Task Force to address the mental health concerns of students and prevent the commission of suicides in Higher Educational Institutions (¶69). The remit of the force is to formulate a comprehensive report on the identification of the predominant causes that lead to the commission of suicides by students, analyse the existing regulatory framework, and recommend measures for strengthening protections (¶74).Prepared by Jemima B SCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: RADHIKA AGARWAL vs UNION OF INDIAW.P.(Crl.) No. 336/2018
Subject: Constitutional validity and mandatory preconditions for the exercise of the power of arrest under the Customs Act and the GST Act
Judgemental Summary: RADHIKA AGARWAL V. UNION OF INDIA2025 INSC 272 (27 February 2025)Justices:  Chief Justice Sanjiv Khanna, Justice M.M. Sundresh, and Justice Bela M. TrivediQuestion(s):  The Supreme Court framed the following issues in a consolidated batch comprising writ petitions, criminal appeals, transferred cases, and a civil appeal challenging the power of arrest under the Customs Act, 1962 (“Customs Act”) and the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (“GST Act”):1.  Whether the power of arrest under Section 104 of the Customs Act and Section 69 of the GST Act is constitutionally valid and what preconditions must be satisfied before such power is exercised?2.  Whether customs officers and GST officers are “police officers” within the meaning of Section 25 of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872?3.  Whether the ratio in Om Prakash v. Union of India [(2011) 14 SCC 1], requiring customs officers to obtain a Magistrate’s warrant before arrest, survives the amendments to the Customs Act in 2012, 2013 and 2019?4.  Whether Sections 69 and 70 of the GST Act are constitutionally valid and whether Parliament had legislative competence under Article 246-A of the Constitution to enact arrest and penal provisions as part of the GST legislation?Factual Background:  These consolidated matters arose from challenges to the exercise of the power of arrest conferred upon customs officers under the Customs Act and upon GST officers under the GST Act. The petitioners questioned whether the provisions of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (“Code”) applied to such arrests, and whether the ratio in Om Prakash v. Union of India [(2011) 14 SCC 1] remained applicable after subsequent legislative amendments.Before Om Prakash, offences under the Customs Act were treated as non-bailable and accused persons were detained for months before being released on bail. Om Prakash held the offences to be non-cognizable and bailable, requiring customs officers to obtain a Magistrate’s warrant before arrest. The 2012, 2013, and 2019 Amendments to the Customs Act then designated certain offences as cognizable and non-bailable, altering the Om Prakash position. Similarly, the GST Act classified offences under Section 132, with Section 132(4) making most offences non-cognizable and bailable, and Section 132(5) designating certain categories as cognizable and non-bailable. The petitioners alleged that officers were coercing assessees into paying tax under threat of arrest, before any assessment or adjudication.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Supreme Court, in a judgment authored by Chief Justice Khanna (for himself and Justice Sundresh), upheld the constitutional validity of the arrest provisions under the Customs Act and the GST Act, rejected the constitutional challenges on all grounds, and laid down preconditions and procedural obligations for the exercise of the arrest power (¶77). The Court held that the ratio in Om Prakash [(2011) 14 SCC 1] was no longer applicable and drew upon the principles in Arvind Kejriwal v. Directorate of Enforcement [(2025) 2 SCC 248] (¶22, ¶46).Justice Trivedi wrote a separate concurring opinion, agreeing with Chief Justice Khanna on when and how the power of arrest should be exercised, and additionally addressing the scope and limitations of judicial review under Articles 32 and 226 of the Constitution when arrest of a person is challenged (¶1, J. Trivedi).Reasons for the Decision:  Inapplicability of Om Prakash and Customs Act Amendments1.  The Supreme Court held that Om Prakash [(2011) 14 SCC 1] was no longer applicable, as the legislature had accepted the ratio of Om Prakash and made specific amendments to the Customs Act (¶4, ¶22). Section 104(4) of the Customs Act, inserted by the 2012 Amendment and expanded by the 2019 Amendment, designated offences relating to prohibited goods, duty evasion exceeding fifty lakh rupees, fraudulent drawback or exemption from duty claims, and fraudulently obtained instruments as cognizable, while Section 104(5) kept all remaining offences non-cognizable (¶7, ¶8). The 2013 Amendment inserted Section 104(6), making specified categories of offences non-bailable, with the 2019 Amendment adding another clause thereto, and Section 104(7) keeping all other offences bailable (¶9–11). The petitioners’ reliance on Om Prakash was therefore no longer valid and had to be rejected (¶22).Customs Officers are Not Police Officers2.  The Supreme Court held that customs officers are not police officers within the meaning of Section 25 of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872, affirming Constitution Bench decisions in Ramesh Chandra Mehta v. State of West Bengal [(1969) 2 SCR 461] and Illias v. Collector of Customs [(1969) 2 SCR 613], and the majority judgment in Tofan Singh v. State of Tamil Nadu [(2021) 4 SCC 1], which held that an officer is deemed a police officer only if conferred with all powers of a police officer, including the power to file a report under Section 173 of the Code (¶14–16).Preconditions for Arrest under the Customs Act3.  The Supreme Court held that the power of arrest under Section 104(1) of the Customs Act is governed by preconditions analogous to those under Section 19(1) of the Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 (“PML Act”), as interpreted in Arvind Kejriwal [(2025) 2 SCC 248] (¶39). The arresting officer must have ‘reasons to believe’ that the person has committed an offence under Sections 132, 133, 135, 135-A, or 136 of the Customs Act (¶40). While Section 104(1) does not explicitly require the officer to have ‘material in their possession’, the officer cannot conclude that an offence has been committed out of thin air or mere suspicion (¶40, ¶43). The threshold is higher than Section 41 of the Code, which allows arrest where a reasonable complaint has been made, or credible information has been received, or a reasonable suspicion exists that the person has committed a cognizable offence; Section 104(1) requires sufficient cause for belief (¶43). The “reasons to believe” must also establish whether the offence is cognizable or non-cognizable, bailable or non-bailable, and whether the monetary thresholds under Sections 104(4) and 104(6) are satisfied (¶44). The “reasons to believe” must be furnished to the arrested person; redaction is permissible only with court approval (¶32, ¶46). The grounds of arrest must be communicated in writing before the person is produced before the Magistrate under Section 104(2), as required by Article 22(1) of the Constitution (¶47).Procedural Obligations on Arresting Officers4.  The Supreme Court held that several provisions of the Code apply to Customs Act arrests, complementing the Customs Act (¶29). Section 41-B requires the officer to display clear identification, prepare an attested arrest memorandum, and inform the arrestee of the right to have a relative or friend notified (¶25). Section 41-D grants the arrested person the right to meet an advocate during interrogation, though not throughout; the advocate may be within visual but not hearing distance, per Senior Intelligence Officer, Directorate of Revenue Intelligence v. Jugal Kishore Samra [(2011) 12 SCC 362] (¶26). Section 50-A requires the officer to forthwith notify a person nominated by the arrestee, and compliance must be recorded in the diary and verified by the Magistrate (¶27). Section 55-A casts a duty of reasonable care for the health and safety of the person in custody (¶28). Customs officers must also maintain duly paginated case diaries under Section 172(1B) of the Code, recording the informant, the person who violated the law, nature of information, time of arrest, seizure details, and statements (¶23, ¶24).Judicial Review of Arrests5.  The Supreme Court held that courts may judicially review the legality of arrests under the Customs Act and the GST Acts, both before and after prosecution complaints are filed, though judicial review does not extend to a merits review or mini-trial (¶33, ¶36). Permissible facets of review include: whether the facts found to exist are supported by any evidence at all (or whether the finding is so perverse that no reasonable person could say the facts exist); whether the conclusion logically flows from the facts; whether jurisdictional terms have been misapplied; whether the power was exercised improperly; and whether relevant grounds were ignored or irrelevant grounds considered (¶37).Justice Trivedi, concurring, held that courts must be extremely loath to exercise judicial review when arrests under special statutes are challenged (¶9, J. Trivedi). Judicial intervention is warranted only when the arrest is prima facie mala fide, prompted by extraneous circumstances, made in contravention of statutory provisions, or when the authority does not have the requisite authority (¶4, J. Trivedi). The sufficiency or adequacy of the material underlying the “reasons to believe” is not open to scrutiny at such a nascent stage (¶10, J. Trivedi), and judicial review may not be exercised unless there is manifest arbitrariness or gross violation or non-compliance of the statutory safeguards (¶13, J. Trivedi).Preconditions for Arrest under the GST Acts6.  The Supreme Court held that, to a large extent, the reasoning and ratio on the applicability of the Code to the Customs Act would equally apply to the GST Acts (¶50). The Commissioner must record reasons to believe, supported by material and evidence, that the person has committed an offence under clauses (a) to (d) of Section 132(1), and that the amount involved exceeds five hundred lakh rupees under clause (i) of Section 132(1); arrest cannot be made merely to investigate whether these conditions are met (¶55–57). A formal assessment order under Section 73 is not invariably required, but the Commissioner must ascertain the conditions of Section 132(5) with sufficient certainty from the material available, applying the benefit of doubt (¶59). The Court noted Instruction No. 02/2022-23 and Instruction No. 01/2025-GST of the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs, regarding guidelines for arrest, and directed compliance with these instructions alongside D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal [(1997) 1 SCC 416] (¶61, ¶62).Prohibition on Coerced Payments7.  The Supreme Court held that recovery of tax under the GST Acts can be made only after issuance of notice and an adjudication order under Section 79 of the GST Acts, and that voluntary payment under Section 74(5) does not authorise coercive extraction or threats of arrest (¶65, ¶67). The Court found force in the petitioners’ submission that the Revenue’s data indicated a correlation between the non-exercise of arrest power and the making of payments, and held that coercion or threats of arrest for recovery of tax would violate the rule of law, and that assessees could seek a refund of tax deposited under threat (¶67, ¶68).Anticipatory Bail and Constitutional Validity of Sections 69 and 708.  The Supreme Court reaffirmed, following Gurbaksh Singh Sibbia v. State of Punjab [(1980) 2 SCC 565] and the Constitution Bench decision in Sushila Aggarwal v. State (NCT of Delhi) [(2020) 5 SCC 1], that anticipatory bail is available even before an FIR is filed; certain contrary decisions in the GST context were held not to be binding (¶70). The constitutional challenge to Sections 69 and 70 of the GST Act on grounds of lack of legislative competence was rejected. The Supreme Court held that Article 246-A of the Constitution, construed in light of the doctrine of pith and substance, is a comprehensive provision empowering Parliament to enact all provisions ancillary and incidental to the levy and collection of GST, including powers to summon, arrest, and prosecute; the vires challenge accordingly failed (¶73–75).Prepared by Sahil RatheeCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: SUNIL KUMAR SINGH vs BIHAR LEGISLATIVE COUNCILW.P.(C) No. 530/2024
Subject: Challenge to the expulsion of a member of the legislature
Judgemental Summary: SUNIL KUMAR SINGH V. BIHAR LEGISLATIVE COUNCIL2025 INSC 264 (25 February 2025)Justices:  Justice Surya Kant, Justice Nongmeukapam K. SinghQuestion(s):  (i) Whether the Writ Petition filed by Mr. Sunil Kumar (“Petitioner”) challenging his expulsion from the Bihar Legislative Council (“BLC”)  is maintainable in view of Article 212 (1) of the Constitution of India and whether the proceedings of the Ethics Committee are amenable to judicial review. (ii) Can the Supreme Court in exercise of its writ jurisdiction review the proportionality of the punishment imposed by the House? (iii) If so, whether the Petitioner’s expulsion is disproportionate to the misconduct attributed to him and whether it merits any interference? (iv) Whether the Supreme  Court is empowered to determine the quantum of punishment that may be imposed on the Petitioner?Factual Background:  The Petitioner, a member of the Rashtriya Janata Dal (“RJD”), filed a Writ Petition under Article 32 of the Constitution of India to challenge his expulsion from the Bihar Legislative Council (“BLC”). The Petitioner was alleged of unparliamentary conduct during the Governor’s address on 13 February  2024. Specifically, he and another Member of the Legislative Council (“MLC”), Md. Sohaib, disrupted the proceedings by using derogatory slogans and mimicking the Chief Minister. A formal complaint was lodged against them on 19 February  2024, leading the BLC Chairman to refer the matter to the Ethics Committee for inquiry. The Ethics Committee scheduled an inquiry for 3 May  2024, which Md. Sohaib attended, expressing regret. However, the Petitioner repeatedly sought exemptions, claiming  engagements related to the Lok Sabha elections and other commitments.He finally appeared before the Ethics Committee on 12 June 2024, but challenged the committee's authority rather than addressing the charges. The Ethics Committee then pre-poned their meeting from 19 June 2024 to 14 June 2024, without notifying the Petitioner, and concluded the proceedings. The Ethics Committee submitted its report on 14 June  2024, recommending the Petitioner's expulsion and Md. Sohaib's suspension for the first two days of the 208th Session of the House.On 26 July  2024, the BLC adopted the report, leading to the Petitioner’s expulsion and Md. Sohaib’s suspension. Subsequently, on 26 July 2024, the BLC Secretariat issued a notification formally relieving the Petitioner of his membership. During the petition's pendency, the Election Commission of India declared a bye-election for the vacant seat on 30 December  2024, with the process to conclude by 25 January 2025. The Supreme Court then stayed the declaration of the bye-election result on 15 January  2025.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Division Bench (two judges) of the Supreme Court quashed the punishment of  expulsion of the Petitioner from the BLC for being disproportionate and excessive.The Court held that the  prohibition under Article 212(1) only bars Constitutional Courts from reviewing the legislative proceedings for procedural irregularity. However, it does not prevent the judicial review of the legality or constitutionality of the legislature's actions (both lawmaking and administrative decisions). The judgement of the Court was authored by Justice Kant. Reasons for the Decision:  The Division Bench (two judges) of the Supreme Court quashed the punishment of  expulsion of the Petitioner from the BLC for being disproportionate and excessive.The Court held that the  prohibition under Article 212(1) only bars Constitutional Courts from reviewing the legislative proceedings for procedural irregularity. However, it does not prevent the judicial review of the legality or constitutionality of the legislature's actions (both lawmaking and administrative decisions). The judgement of the Court was authored by Justice Kant. Reasons for the Decision:Maintainability of Writ Petition under Article 212(1) and Judicial Review of Ethics Committee ProceedingsThe Supreme Court rejected the objection to the maintainability of the writ petition under Article 212(1) of the Constitution, which bars judicial scrutiny of procedural irregularities in “Proceedings in the Legislature”. The Court reasoned that Article 212(1) protects only the formal steps and debates within the House (motions, deliberations etc.) from judicial inquiry but does not shield legislative decisions or administrative actions (¶¶10-14, ¶23). The Ethics Committee’s recommendation for the Petitioner’s expulsion was deemed an administrative function under Rules framed under Article 208, aimed at enforcing discipline, and not a “proceeding” or “legislative decision” (¶¶17-18). The Court held that  such administrative actions, even by legislative bodies, are subject to judicial review if they infringe fundamental rights, lack constitutional authority, or are arbitrary (¶¶19-20). The Court ruled that expulsion impacted the  fundamental rights of the petitioner and representative governance, necessitating judicial scrutiny of its legality and proportionality (¶¶21-22).Proportionality of Punishment Imposed by the HouseThe Supreme Court held that Constitutional Courts can examine the proportionality of punishments imposed by legislative bodies. The doctrine of proportionality requires that punitive measures be necessary, balanced, and the least restrictive means to achieve disciplinary objectives (¶¶25-26, ¶62). The Court reasoned that expulsion, being a grave measure, must be reserved for exceptional cases and account for its impact on democratic representation (¶¶57-59). The Court outlined guiding factors for assessing proportionality, including: (i) the degree of obstruction caused by the member; (ii) whether the conduct harmed the House’s dignity; (iii) the member’s prior and subsequent conduct (remorse); (iv) availability of lesser penalties; and (v) balancing societal interests, such as the electorate’s right to representation (¶64). The Court held that while Constitutional Courts must defer to legislative autonomy, they must intervene if the punishment is manifestly disproportionate or shocks constitutional conscience (¶61).Power of the Supreme Court to Determine Quantum of Punishment under Article 142The Supreme Court reasoned  that while judicial restraint ordinarily requires courts to remit matters of punishment to disciplinary authorities, Article 142 empowers it to substitute the punishment in exceptional cases to ensure “complete justice” (¶¶79-81). The Court ruled   that remanding the matter to the Ethics Committee for reconsideration would result in protracted proceedings, depriving the Petitioner of participation in legislative sessions and exacerbating the infringement of his fundamental rights under Article 14 (¶¶82-83). The Court found that the Petitioner had already undergone seven months of expulsion, with his term set to expire in 2026, leaving limited time for representation (¶82).The Supreme  Court therefore modified the punishment by Invoking Article 142, deeming the period of expulsion already served as equivalent to suspension, which it held was proportionate to the misconduct (¶86). The Supreme Court reinstated the Petitioner as a member of the Bihar Legislative Council with immediate effect but barred him from claiming remuneration for the expulsion period. The Court clarified that this modification did not condone the Petitioner’s conduct and warned against future misconduct (¶¶86-87). As a result, the Press Note dated 30.12.2024 issued by Election Commission, declaring the bye-election for the seat earlier held by the Petitioner was quashed, and all actions following it were annulled.Prepared by Osama Noor and Raza ZaidiCentre for Research and Planning |  Supreme Court of India © Supreme Court of India

Title: SUKHDEV SINGH vs SUKHBIR KAURC.A. No. 2536/2019
Subject: Right of a spouse to claim interim maintenance and permanent alimony under Sections 24 and 25 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 when a marriage is declared void
Judgemental Summary: SUKHDEV SINGH V. SUKHBIR KAUR2025 INSC 197 (12 February 2025)Justices:  Justice Abhay S. Oka, Justice Ahsanuddin Amanullah and Justice Augustine G. MasihQuestion(s):  1.  Whether a spouse of a marriage declared void by a competent court under Section 11 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (“the 1955 Act”) is entitled to claim permanent alimony and maintenance under Section 25 of the 1955 Act?2.  Whether in a petition filed seeking a declaration under Section 11 of the 1955 Act, a spouse is entitled to seek maintenance pendente lite under Section 24 of the 1955 Act?Factual Background:  A Division Bench of the Supreme Court, by an order dated 22 August 2024, referred the questions to a Three-Judge Bench due to conflicting judicial views on the applicability of Sections 24 and 25 of the 1955 Act in cases of void marriages. Chand Dhawan v. Jawaharlal Dhawan [(1993) 3 SCC 406] and Rameshchandra Rampratapji Daga v. Rameshwari Rameshchandra Daga [(2005) 2 SCC 33]  held that alimony could be granted, while Yamunabai Anantrao Adhav v. Anantrao Shivram Adhav [(1988) 1 SCC 530], Savitaben Somabhai Bhatiya v. State of Gujarat [(2005) 3 SCC 636], and others held to the contrary. The matter was accordingly placed before a Three-Judge Bench.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Supreme Court answered both questions as follows:(a)  A spouse whose marriage has been declared void under Section 11 of the 1955 Act is entitled to seek permanent alimony or maintenance from the other spouse by invoking Section 25 of the 1955 Act. Whether such relief can be granted depends on the facts of each case and the conduct of the parties, as the grant of relief under Section 25 is always discretionary.(b)  Even if a court arrives at a prima facie conclusion that the marriage is void or voidable, pending the final disposal of the proceedings under the 1955 Act, the court is not precluded from granting maintenance pendente lite provided the conditions under Section 24 are satisfied. The grant of relief under Section 24 is also always discretionary and the Court will take into consideration the conduct of the party seeking relief.The Court directed the Registry to place the appeals before the appropriate Bench for decision on merits.Reasons for the Decision:  Interpretation of “Any Decree” in Section 251.  The Court held that Section 25 of the 1955 Act confers power on a matrimonial court to grant permanent alimony “at the time of passing any decree or at any time subsequent thereto.” The 1955 Act contemplates the following decrees: a decree under Section 9 (restitution of conjugal rights), Section 10 (judicial separation), Section 11 (void marriage), Section 12 (voidable marriage) and Sections 13 and 13B (divorce) (¶16). Since the legislature drew no distinction between a decree of divorce and a decree of nullity while enacting Section 25(1), a decree of nullity under Section 11 could not be excluded from the purview of Section 25(1) (¶18). The Court affirmed Chand Dhawan v. Jawaharlal Dhawan and Rameshchandra Rampratapji Daga v. Rameshwari Rameshchandra Daga, which held that “at the time of passing of any decree” encompasses all decrees under the 1955 Act, and further held that entitlement under Section 25 does not depend on whether the void marriage was moral or immoral, as Section 25 exists to prevent a financially dependent spouse from being rendered destitute (¶¶19-20).Distinguishing Earlier Decisions2.  The Court held that Yamunabai Anantrao Adhav v. Anantrao Shivram Adhav and Savitaben Somabhai Bhatiya v. State of Gujarat both involved proceedings under Section 125 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (“CrPC”), which operates through a summary procedure in a different field and had no application to Section 25 of the 1955 Act (¶¶21, 25). Abbayolla Reddy v. Padmamma [AIR 1999 AP 19] rested on Section 18 of the Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956, a different provision, while Navdeep Kaur v. Dilraj Singh [(2003) 1 HLR 100] gave an unduly narrow reading to “decree in proceedings,” contrary to the view in Chand Dhawan v. Jawaharlal Dhawan (¶23).3.  The Court held that the Full Bench of the Bombay High Court in Bhausaheb @ Sandhu S/o Raghuji Magar v. Leelabai W/o Bhausaheb Magar [(2004) AIR Bom. 283(FB)] had erred in law. The use of the terms “illegitimate wife” and “faithful mistress” to describe a woman who is a party to a void marriage was held to be misogynistic and a violation of Article 21 of the Constitution of India, which guarantees the right to a dignified life (¶24).Discretionary Nature of Relief4.  The Court held that the grant of relief under Section 25 is discretionary, as the provision uses the word “may.” If the conduct of the spouse seeking maintenance is such that they are not entitled to discretionary relief, the court can always decline the prayer. Equitable considerations and the conduct of the parties are always relevant (¶26).Section 24: Maintenance Pendente Lite5.  The Court held that Section 24 applies to any proceeding pending under the 1955 Act. Its conditions are: (i) a proceeding under the 1955 Act must be pending; and (ii) the court must conclude that either the wife or the husband has no independent income sufficient for their support and the necessary expenses of the proceeding (¶27). Even if a court prima facie finds the marriage to be void or voidable, it is not precluded from granting maintenance pendente lite if these conditions are satisfied. The grant of relief under Section 24 is also discretionary and the conduct of the party seeking relief will always be considered (¶28).Prepared by Sahil RatheeCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: VIHAAN KUMAR vs THE STATE OF HARYANACrl.A. No. 621/2025
Subject: Consequence of not informing grounds of arrest
Judgemental Summary: VIHAAN KUMAR V. THE STATE OF HARYANA2025 INSC 162 (7 February 2025)Justices:  Justice Abhay S. Oka, Justice Nongmeikapam Kotiswar SinghQuestion(s):  (i) Whether the Appellant’s arrest was rendered illegal due to non-compliance with Article 22(1) of the Constitution (failure to communicate grounds of arrest). (ii) Whether the Appellant’s treatment (handcuffing and chaining to a hospital bed) violated his fundamental right to dignity under Article 21. (iii) Whether subsequent legal processes (remand, chargesheet) can validate an arrest vitiated by constitutional violations.Factual Background:  Vihaan Kumar (“Appellant”), was arrested on 10 June 2024 for offences under Sections 409, 420, 467, 468, and 471 read with Section 120-B of the Indian Penal Code. The Appellant filed a writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution  before the Punjab and Haryana High Court challenging his arrest as illegal, which was dismissed on 30 August 2024.  Consequently, he appealed before the Supreme Court. The Appellant claimed that he was arrested at 10:30 AM on 10 June 2024 and produced before a magistrate on 11 June 2024 at 3:30 PM, alleging a violation of Article 22(2) (failure to produce the accused before a Magistrate within 24 hours). He further contended that the grounds of arrest were not communicated to him, breaching Article 22(1). The State, however, asserted that the arrest occurred at 6:00 PM on 10 June 2024, complying with the 24-hour rule, and that the grounds were orally communicated.A critical additional grievance arose when the Appellant, after arrest, was hospitalized at PGIMS, Rohtak, where he was handcuffed and chained to a hospital bed. Photographic evidence corroborated this treatment, prompting the Supreme Court to issue notices to the hospital and police authorities. The State later suspended the involved officers and initiated a departmental inquiry.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, declaring the Appellant’s arrest illegal due to non-compliance with Article 22(1). The Court ordered the Appellant’s immediate release, quashed all subsequent remand orders, and directed the State of Haryana to issue guidelines prohibiting the handcuffing of accused persons in hospitals. The judgment of the Court was authored by Justice Oka. Justice Singh authored a concurring opinion.Reasons for the Decision:  Violation of Article 22(1): Failure to Communicate Grounds of ArrestThe Supreme Court held  that Article 22(1) mandates that an arrested person be informed of the grounds of arrest “as soon as may be,” ensuring the ability to challenge detention and seek legal remedies. The Court referred to Pankaj Bansal v. Union of India 2023 INSC 866 and Prabir Purkayastha v. State (NCT of Delhi) 2024 INSC 414 and held  that communication of grounds must be meaningful and effective, preferably in writing, to avoid disputes. While Article 22(1) does not explicitly require written communication, the Court ruled that vague assertions or oral claims by police are insufficient (¶¶11-14). The Appellant’s petition specifically pleaded non-communication of grounds of arrest, which the State failed to counter in its affidavits. Instead, the State claimed the Appellant’s wife was informed, which the Supreme Court dismissed as irrelevant to constitutional compliance (¶¶22-24). The State’s reliance on a case diary entry (recording communication of grounds at 6:10 PM) was rejected by the Court as an “afterthought” due to the absence of contemporaneous documentation of the actual grounds (¶27). The Court reasoned that the burden to prove compliance lies on the State, and mere diary entries without detailing the grounds were deemed inadequate. The Court thus held that non-compliance with Article 22(1) vitiated the arrest, rendering all subsequent custody unlawful (¶22-28). Violation of Article 21: Handcuffing and Chaining to Hospital BedThe Supreme Court condemned the Appellant’s treatment as a gross violation of dignity under Article 21. The Court found the handcuffing and chaining to a hospital bed without justification shocking and unconstitutional. The Court directed the State to issue guidelines to prevent such practices, emphasizing that custodial treatment must respect human dignity (¶29). Effect of Subsequent Legal ProcessesThe State argued that the filing of a chargesheet validated the Appellant’s custody. The Supreme  Court rejected this and held that constitutional violations at the arrest stage cannot be cured by subsequent procedures. The Court reasoned that once an arrest is vitiated under Article 22(1), continued detention based on remand orders or trial proceedings remains unlawful (¶16).Justice Singh in his concurring opinion, reinforced Justice Oka’s conclusions while elaborating on the constitutional and statutory imperatives underpinning the communication of grounds of arrest. He reasoned  that the mandate of Article 22(1) that requires arrested persons to be informed of the grounds of arrest is not merely procedural but a fundamental safeguard to protect liberty under Article 21. Justice Singh  ruled  that written communication of grounds is essential to ensure the arrestee can meaningfully challenge detention and seek legal remedies. Justice Singh further noted that statutory provisions such as Section 50 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (“CrPC”)  (Section 47 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023) and Section 19 of the Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 operationalize this constitutional right, making non-compliance a fatal illegality (J. Singh ¶2).Justice Singh expanded on the role of Section 50A of the CrPC, which obligates authorities to inform the arrestee’s relatives or nominated persons about the arrest and its grounds. He reasoned that this requirement  was not a “mere formality” but a critical mechanism to enable the arrestee’s circle to act swiftly such as arranging legal aid or bail to actualize the right to liberty. By linking this statutory duty to Article 21, Justice Singh held that the failure to communicate grounds in writing to both the arrestee and their representatives undermines the very purpose of constitutional safeguards, rendering the arrest unlawful (J. Singh ¶3).Prepared by Osama Noor Centre for Research and Planning |  Supreme Court of India © Supreme Court of India

Title: TANVI BEHL vs SHREY GOELC.A. No. 9289/2019
Subject: Constitutional validity of domicile/residence-based reservation for admissions to Post Graduate (PG) Medical Courses within the State Quota
Judgemental Summary: TANVI BEHL V. SHREY GOEL2025 INSC 125 (29 January 2025)Justices:  Justice Hrishikesh Roy, Justice Sudhanshu Dhulia, Justice Sarasa V N BhattiQuestion(s):  (i) Whether providing for domicile/residence-based reservation in admission to PG Medical Courses within the State Quota is constitutionally invalid and is impermissible?(ii) If such reservation is permissible, what should be the extent and manner of providing it within State Quota seats, and what should be the modality for a State/UT having only one Medical College?(iii) If such reservation is impermissible, how are the State Quota seats, other than the permissible institutional preference seats, to be filled up?Factual Background:  The case originates from the Union Territory of Chandigarh, which has only a single medical institution. The admission process for PG Medical Courses in 2019 commenced with sixty four seats allocated to the State Quota. The college prospectus distributed these sixty four seats equally: Thirty two seats for the Institutional Preference Pool, for candidates who passed MBBS from the same college, and Thirty-two seats were allocated to the UT Chandigarh Pool. Eligibility for the UT Chandigarh Pool was based on broad criteria, including a candidate having studied in Chandigarh, or their parents having resided in or held immovable property in Chandigarh for a period of five years at any time prior to the application.The Punjab and Haryana High Court declared the residence-based criteria invalid and unconstitutional, finding they violated Article 14 and had no rationale to the objects sought to be achieved. Consequently, the college was directed to fill those seats based on NEET merit. This decision was subsequently challenged before the Supreme Court, which initially granted an interim stay.Decision of the Supreme Court:  The Supreme Court held that providing for domicile or residence-based reservation in PG Medical Courses is constitutionally invalid and impermissible. While upholding the thirty two seats for institutional preference, the Court affirmed the High Court’s finding that the thirty two residence-based seats were wrongly filled. However, the Court ruled that students already admitted or who have completed their courses would not be affected due to the equity in their favor. The judgment was authored by Justice Dhulia.Reasons for the Decision:  I. The Concept of a Single Indian DomicileThe Supreme Court emphasized that the Indian Constitution recognizes only one domicile, namely, domicile in the territory of India (¶19). The Court observed that the concept of regional or provincial domicile is alien to the Indian legal system.(¶19) It is a total misapprehension of the position in law to speak of a person being domiciled in a specific State or Province.(¶22) Referring to Dr. Pradeep Jain v. Union of India (1984 INSC 117) the Court condemned the practice of State Governments using the term domicile loosely to mean permanent residence, cautioning that such misuse could give rise to lethal radiations that tend to break up the national unity.(¶21)II. Violation of Equality under Articles 14 and 15The Supreme Court reasoned that residence-based reservation constitutes an invasion on the fundamental rights of several students, who are treated unequally solely on the ground that they belong to a different State in the Union and runs counter to the basic principle of equality before the law (¶31). It further observed that although Article 15 does not explicitly list residence as a prohibited ground of discrimination, any requirement based on residence must nevertheless satisfy the test of Article 14 (¶28).III. Distinction Between Undergraduate (MBBS) and Post-Graduate (PG) CoursesThe Supreme Court recognised that in MBBS courses, a limited degree of residence-based reservation may be permissible to address local needs, the backwardness of an area, and the State's expenditure on infrastructure (¶29). However, the same logic does not apply to PG Medical Courses. Citing Jagadish Saran v. Union of India (1980 INSC 11), the Court held that at the level of PhD or MD, where an international measure of talent is required, the role of reservation must diminish (¶29). The Court emphasised that merit must be the test when choosing the best, as devaluing merit at the summit risks stunting the nation's development in vital professional expertise (¶30).IV. Adherence to Judicial PrecedentThe decision rests on a long line of settled law, primarily the decisions in Jagadish Saran v. Union of India (1980 INSC 11), Dr. Pradeep Jain v. Union of India (1984 INSC 117), and the Constitution Bench judgment in Saurabh Chaudri v. Union of India (2003 INSC 597) (¶6). These precedents collectively establish that while institutional preference may constitute a reasonable classification, residence-based reservation in PG Medical courses is constitutionally impermissible ( ¶¶ 32-33).Prepared by Jemima B SCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: AJAY MALIK vs STATE OF UTTARAKHANDCrl.A. No. 441/2025
Subject: Constitution of Committee to Formulate Legal Framework for Protection of Domestic Workers
Judgemental Summary: AJAY MALIK V. STATE OF UTTARAKHAND2025 INSC 118 (29 January 2025)Justices:  Justice Surya Kant and Justice Ujjal BhuyanQuestion(s):  (i) Whether the High Court acted within its powers under Section 482 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (“Cr.P.C.”) in rejecting Appellant’s quashing petition. (ii) Whether the High Court erred in rejecting the compounding application, which was moved by the appellant and supported by the complainant.(iii) Whether the High Court was legally correct in accepting the co-accused’s Criminal Revision and discharging him. (iv) Whether the existing legal framework in India sufficiently protects the rights of domestic workers.Factual Background:  The Complainant, a woman belonging to a financially disadvantaged family, was brought to Delhi by her neighbours (Subhash and Mohan Ram) and handed over to a Placement Agency (operated by Shambhu). Appellant herein (Ajay Malik) recruited her as a domestic worker for his Dehradun residence in October 2016 through this agency. During the absence of Appellant, the complainant was left at the residence with the main door locked, and a spare key was entrusted to a neighbor (Ashok Kumar). The complainant contacted the police using a phone left by Appellant, leading to her recovery. An FIR was registered against Ajay Malik, Mohan Ram, Subhash, and Shambhu under Sections 343 and 370 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (“IPC”) and subsequently charged under section 120B of the IPC vide second chargesheet. Third chargesheet later added Ashok Kumar as a co-accused under Sections 343 and 120B of the IPC. The High Court subsequently rejected Appellant’s applications to quash or compound the proceedings, but discharged co-accused. Hence, the Appellant approached the Supreme Court in the present proceedings and State approached against discharge of the co-accused in connected appeal.Decision of the Supreme Court:  A Two-Judge Bench of the Supreme Court allowed the appeal, setting aside the High Court’s judgment and quashing the criminal proceedings against Appellant. The Supreme Court dismissed the State’s appeal against the co-accused, upholding his discharge. Furthermore, the Supreme Court directed the Government of India to constitute a Committee comprising subject experts to formulate a legal framework for the protection and regulation of domestic workers. The judgment of the Court was authored by Justice Surya Kant.Reasons for the Decision:  No Prima Facie Case of Wrongful Confinement The Supreme Court held that the allegations do not disclose the commission of an offence of wrongful confinement under section 343 of IPC (¶17). The Supreme Court reasoned that while the main entry to the house was locked, an alternative unlocked exit was freely available to the Complainant as also illustrated in the site plan (¶15) and also the Complainant’s own no-objection affidavits stated that she was never wrongfully confined and an alternative exit was always accessible to her.  Furthermore, the fact that Appellant left a mobile phone with her and Complainant possessed a temporary pass to leave the DRDO colony contradicted the claims of confinement. (¶16)No Evidence of Trafficking or Exploitation by Malik The Supreme Court held that no prima facie offence under Section 370 of the IPC qua the Appellant has been made out (¶20). The Supreme Court noted that the primary allegations of abuse, exploitation, and withholding of wages were directed at other co-accused, not the Appellant (¶18). Further, the complainant clarified in her additional affidavits that the Appellant did not traffic or wrongfully confine her, and she sought to recant all allegations against him (¶19).Lack of Criminal Conspiracy The Supreme Court rejected the charge under section 120B of the IPC qua Appellant as being highly speculative. The Supreme found no evidence of an orchestrated agreement between Appellant and the co-accused to exploit the Complainant. Their interactions were strictly limited to the hiring process of the Complainant as a domestic help (¶24).Justification for QuashingThe Supreme Court quashed the FIR, chargesheet and subsequent proceedings (¶55(i)). The Supreme Court held that proceeding to trial would be an exercise in futility and a sheer wastage of judicial resources (¶26) given the absence of a prima facie case, the vague and omnibus claims made in FIR (¶29) transcribed mechanically into the chargesheet, and the Complainant’s desire for proceeding against Appellant to be quashed. (¶¶ 26, 28)Correctness of rejecting Appellant’s Compounding ApplicationThe Supreme Court noted that although it was inclined to agree with the High Court’s rejection of Appellant’s Compounding Application owing to the non-compoundable nature of the alleged offences, the issue was rendered academic given the Court’s prior conclusion to quash the proceedings. Hence, the correctness of the High Court’s decision to disallow the Compounding Application did not require further consideration on merits. However, the Supreme Court underscored that a delicate balance ought to be struck in cases wherein parties seek compounding of offences and cautioned that an excessively moralistic order, though well-intentioned, may unnecessarily prolong criminal proceedings that have no logical conclusion and only serve to further distress the parties (¶32).Validity of Ashok Kumar’s Discharge The Supreme Court upheld Ashok Kumar’s discharge (¶55(ii)) as there were no direct allegations against him by the Complainant, he was added to the chargesheet merely as an afterthought, and there was a demonstrable lack of mens rea or intent to aid in any confinement since he only held the keys as a favor. (¶¶35, 36)Addressing the Legal Vacuum for Domestic Workers The Supreme Court observed that the reason for harassment and rampant abuse is the legal vacuum for rights and protection of domestic workers (¶41). The Supreme Court emphasized that domestic workers remain highly vulnerable to exploitation despite international treaties like the ILO’s Domestic Workers Convention, 2011 advocating for their rights (¶¶41,45). The Supreme Court exercised its parens patriae jurisdiction to intervene while noting the repeated failure of several legislative Bills to materialize into law (¶¶49,53) and directed various ministries (Ministry of Labour and Employment in tandem with the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment, the Ministry of Women and Child Development, and the Ministry of Law and Justice) to form a committee to propose a legal framework, preferably within 6 months (¶55).Prepared by Nikhil Kumar ChaubeyCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: RAJEEB KALITA vs UNION OF INDIAW.P.(C) No. 538/2023
Subject: Directions for ensuring adequate and accessible toilet facilities in courts and tribunals
Judgemental Summary: RAJEEB KALITA V. UNION OF INDIA2025 INSC 75 (15 January 2025)Justices:  Justice Jamshed B. Pardiwala and Justice R. MahadevanQuestion(s):  (i) Whether provision of clean and hygienic toilets in court premises is necessary to secure the right under Article 21.Factual Background:  The petitioner, a practising advocate, filed the present Public Interest Litigation under Article 32. He sought a writ of mandamus directing all States and Union Territories to ensure that basic toilet facilities are made available in all courts and tribunals across the country for men, women, transgender persons and persons with disabilities. It was further prayed that the same should be identifiable and accessible by the advocates/ litigants/ court staff etc. The petitioner submitted that clean and hygienic toilets are essential not only at public places such as markets, railway stations, tourist spots and office complexes, but also within court complexes where advocates, litigants and staff are present in large numbers. In the absence of such facilities, it was contended, the right guaranteed under Article 21 would be rendered meaningless.By an earlier order, the Supreme Court directed all the High Courts to file affidavits furnishing specific details regarding toilet facilities. The Supreme Court observed a lack of concrete statistical data on the availability of toilets for differently abled persons and separate facilities for women judicial officers, alongside inadequate transparency regarding availability of funds and timelines for construction. It also noted the absence of sanitary napkin dispensers and creche facilities for nursing mothers.Decision of the Supreme Court:  A Two-Judge Bench of the Supreme Court held that access to proper sanitation is a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Constitution, and issued directions in the larger public interest. It directed that all High Courts and State Governments/UTs ensure the construction and availability of separate, identifiable, and accessible toilet facilities for males, females, PwD, and transgender persons across all court premises and tribunals. The Supreme Court directed the constitution of a specific committee in each High Court within six weeks to formulate a comprehensive sanitation plan. Furthermore, State Governments and UTs were directed to allocate sufficient separate funds for construction and maintenance, with a status report to be filed within four months. The judgment of the Court was authored by Justice Mahadevan.Reasons for the Decision:  Fundamental Right to Proper SanitationThe Supreme Court emphasized that toilets are not merely a convenience but a basic necessity and a facet of human rights. Access to proper sanitation is recognized as a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Constitution, which inherently includes ensuring a safe and hygienic environment. Furthermore, there is a discernible duty on every State/UT under Part IV of the Constitution to ensure a healthy environment and continuously strive to improve public health. (¶10) The Supreme Court held that it is the duty of the Government and local authorities to provide and maintain hygienic toilet and sanitation facilities within the Court premises for men, women, persons with disabilities (PwD), and transgender persons. (¶8.24)Barrier to Fair Administration of JusticeThe Supreme Court observed that access to justice includes creating a pleasant atmosphere for all stakeholders. Litigants should not be forced to refrain from exercising their legal rights due to the fear of sitting in courts for long hours without access to basic amenities (¶10). The absence of adequate washroom facilities undermines equality and poses a barrier to the fair administration of justice (¶10.2).Directions IssuedThe Supreme Court issued comprehensive directions in larger public interest while directing status report to be filed within period of four months (¶11):(i) All High Courts and State/Union Territory Governments are mandated to ensure the construction and availability of separate toilet facilities for males, females, Persons with Disabilities (PwD), and transgender persons across all Court premises and Tribunals(ii) High Courts shall oversee and ensure that these facilities are clearly identifiable and accessible to Judges, advocates, litigants, and court staff.(iii) A committee shall be constituted in each High Court within six weeks, chaired by a Judge nominated by the Chief Justice and comprising the Registrar General/Registrar of the High Court, Chief Secretary, PWD Secretary, Finance Secretary, a Bar Association representative, and any other officer as deemed fit, to formulate and implement a comprehensive action plan. Furthermore, the committee must carry out following tasks and ensure its implementation:The committees must track daily visitor statistics to ensure sufficient washrooms are built, conduct surveys to assess infrastructure gaps, and provide alternate facilities like mobile or bio-toilets during the construction phase.Washrooms must have clear signage and functional amenities, including water, electricity, hand soap, and toilet paper, with specific requirements for PwD ramps.Must implement mandatory cleaning schedules to keep bathroom floors dry and ensure regular maintenance by outsourcing the upkeep to professional cleaning agencies on a contract basis.A fast-track complaint and redressal system must be framed for the speedy reporting and instant repair of defective washrooms, alongside mechanisms for periodic inspections.A specific nodal officer must be appointed at each court premise to monitor maintenance, address complaints, and issue standing instructions regarding the upkeep of the toilets.Have a transparent and separate monetary fund for the construction and maintenance of toilets in court complexes.Family court complexes must be equipped with child-safe washrooms monitored by trained staff. must provide separate, interconnected rooms for nursing mothers featuring feeding stations and changing platforms, and High Courts are to develop a grading and certification system for District Courts and other subordinate forums to develop and sustain the quality of maintenance.(iv) State Governments and Union Territories must allocate sufficient funds for the construction and maintenance of toilet facilities within the court premises.Prepared by Nikhil Kumar ChaubeyCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: RINA KUMARI @ RINA DEVI @ REENA vs DINESH KUMAR MAHTO @ DINESH KUMAR MAHATOCrl.A. No. 161/2025
Subject: Right of a spouse to claim maintenance under Section 125 Cr.P.C. in cases of non-compliance with a decree of restitution of conjugal rights
Judgemental Summary: RINA KUMARI @ RINA DEVI @ REENA V. DINESH KUMAR MAHTO @ DINESH KUMAR MAHATO2025 INSC 55 (10 January 2025)Justices:  Chief Justice Sanjiv Khanna and Justice Sanjay KumarQuestion(s):  (i) Whether non-compliance with a decree for restitution of conjugal rights by a wife would be sufficient in itself to deny her maintenance.(ii) Whether the findings in judgment of a court in a suit for restitution of conjugal rights binding on a Court deciding a petition for maintenance under Section 125 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (“Cr.P.C.”).Factual Background:  The Appellant (wife) and Respondent (husband) were married in May 2014 and parted ways in August 2015. On 15.02.2022, Family Court (Dhanbad) allowed the application for maintenance filed by the Appellant on 03.08.2019, directing Respondent to pay Rs. 10,000 per month from date of filing of the application. On 23.04.2022, the Family Court (Ranchi), in a separate petition for restitution of conjugal rights filed by the Respondent on 20.07.2018, directed the Appellant to resume conjugal life. The Respondent challenged the order dated 15.02.2022 before Jharkhand High Court, which overturned the maintenance order in the revisional jurisdiction on the reason that because Appellant did not comply with the restitution decree, she was living separately without reasonable excuse, invoking the disqualification under Section 125(4) of the Cr.P.C. Hence, the Appellant approached the Supreme Court in the present proceedings.Decision of the Supreme Court:  A Two-Judge Bench of the Supreme Court allowed the appeal and set aside the High Court’s judgment while restoring the Family Court’s order dated 15.02.2022. The Supreme Court ordered the Respondent to pay maintenance on or before 10th of each calendar month and the arrears of maintenance shall be paid in three instalments.The Supreme Court held that a decree for restitution of conjugal rights secured by the husband, coupled with non-compliance by the wife, would not be determinative of her right to maintenance or the applicability of the disqualification under Section 125(4) Cr.P.C. Furthermore, it was held that findings in a judgment for restitution of conjugal rights by the Family Court, being a civil court, are not binding on the court seized of a petition under Section 125 Cr.P.C.Reasons for the Decision:  Objective of Section 125 Cr.P.C.The Supreme Court reiterated that provisions relating to maintenance are a measure of social justice intended to alleviate the financial hardship of destitute wives, children, and parents, who are left to support themselves (¶10). In such proceedings, the husband cannot be allowed to evade his obligation through subterfuge so as to deny the wife a life of dignity; relief from this obligation is permissible only where a court, on legally sustainable grounds, determines that the wife is not entitled to maintenance. (¶11)Civil nature of Maintenance ProceedingsMaintenance proceedings under Section 125 of the CrPC are essentially civil in nature (¶30), and the rationale for incorporating the relevant provisions relating to the maintenance of wives and children in the Code of Criminal Procedure was to provide a remedy that is faster and more economical than that available through the civil courts as clarified by the Law Commission of India in its 41st Report. (¶9)No Automatic DisqualificationThe Supreme Court held that a decree for restitution of conjugal rights obtained by the husband, even if not complied with by the wife, is not by itself determinative of her entitlement to maintenance or of the applicability of the disqualification under Section 125(4) of the Cr.P.C. A wife may nonetheless be entitled to maintenance if she has a valid and sufficient reason for refusing to reside with her husband, notwithstanding the decree. (¶29)Independence of ProceedingsThe Supreme Court held that the proceedings for restitution of conjugal rights and maintenance under Section 125 of the Cr.P.C. are wholly independent and bear no direct or indirect connection, inasmuch as proceedings under Section 125 of the Cr.P.C. do not arise out of proceedings for restitution of conjugal rights (¶33), and based on this the Court rejected the argument that findings from a civil court in judgment for the restitution of conjugal rights are strictly binding on proceedings under Section 125 Cr.P.C. (¶30)Sufficient Cause to Stay SeparatelyThe Supreme Court held that the Respondent’s complete neglect of the Appellant following her miscarriage, coupled with prior ill-treatment in the matrimonial home, constituted sufficient cause for her to live separately. Furthermore, no attempt at reconciliation had been made since 2017. (¶37)Lack of Bona FidesThe Supreme Court observed that the Respondent made no attempt to execute the restitution decree or seek a divorce, proving that he merely obtained the decree to create a stalemate and shield himself from paying maintenance. (¶¶37, 38) Prepared by Nikhil Kumar ChaubeyCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: MYPREFERRED TRANSFORMATION AND HOSPITALITY PVT. LTD. vs M/S. FARIDABAD IMPLEMENTS PVT. LTD.C.A. No. 336/2025
Subject: Applicability of the Limitation Act, 1963 and Section 10 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 to the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996
Judgemental Summary: MYPREFERRED TRANSFORMATION AND HOSPITALITY PVT. LTD. V. M/S. FARIDABAD IMPLEMENTS PVT. LTD.2025 INSC 56 (10 January 2025)Justices:  Justice Pamidighantam S. Narasimha and Justice Pankaj MithalQuestion(s):  (i) Do the provisions of the Limitation Act, 1963 (“Limitation Act”) apply to Section 34 of Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 (“ACA”) proceedings, and to what extent? (ii) Does Section 4 of the Limitation Act apply to Section 34(3) of ACA as per an analysis of the statutory scheme as well as precedents of this Court on the issue? If Section 4 applies, does it apply only to the 3-month limitation period or also the 30-day condonable period? (iii) In light of the answer in (ii), will Section 10 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 (“GCA”) apply to Section 34(3) of ACA, and if so, in what manner?Factual Background:  The appellants received a signed hard copy of an arbitral award on 14.02.2022 and a scanned copy of award by email on 04.02.2022. The 3-month limitation period for filing an application under Section 34(3) of the ACA, after considering the extension granted by this Court’s COVID-19 pandemic order In Re: Cognizance of Extension of Limitation dated 10.01.2022, expired on 29.05.2022, which was a working day for the court. The further 30-day condonable period expired on 28.06.2022, a date that fell during the High Court’s summer vacation spanning from 04.06.2022 to 03.07.2022,. The appellants filed their Section 34 petition along with an application for condonation of delay on 04.07.2022, which was the first day the court reopened. The single judge and the division bench of the High Court dismissed the petition under Section 34 and Section 37 of ACA respectively as being barred by limitation. Hence, the Appellants filed the present Appeal before the Supreme Court.Decision of the Supreme Court:  A Two-Judge Bench of the Supreme Court dismissed the appeal and affirmed the judgment of the High Court, concluding that the application under section 34 of ACA was barred by limitation. The Supreme Court held that the petition was filed beyond the condonable period of 30 days, which conclusively and absolutely expired on 28.06.2022, and Section 4 of the Limitation Act does not apply when the 30-day condonable period expires on a court holiday and Section 10 of GCA stands excluded due to application of the Limitation Act. The judgment of the court was authored by Justice Narasimha. Justice Mithal authored a concurring opinion.Reasons for the Decision:  Applicability of the Limitation Act to ACA The Supreme Court while relying upon Mukri Gopalan v. Cheppilat Puthanpurayil (1995) 5 SCC 5 and Consolidated Engineering Enterprises v. Principal Secretary, Irrigation Department [(2008) 7 SCC 169] held that by virtue of Section 29(2) of the Limitation Act applies to court proceedings under the ACA  and the purpose of Section 43(1) of the ACA is to extend the applicability of Limitation Act to arbitrations proceedings under the ACA unless expressly excluded (¶¶8-11). In his concurring opinion, Justice Mithal fully agreed, emphasizing that Section 43 of the ACA unequivocally makes the Limitation Act applicable to arbitration proceedings (¶8 J. Mithal). The Supreme Court reiterated that mere prescription of a period of limitation that is different from the Limitation Act is not sufficient to displace the applicability of Limitation Act's provisions. Furthermore, the Court can examine the language of the special law and its scheme to conclude that certain provisions of the Limitation Act are impliedly excluded (¶16).Applicability of the Limitation Act to Section 34(3)The Supreme Court held that there is no wholesale exclusion of Sections 4 to 24 of the Limitation Act when calculating the period of limitation under Section 34(3) of the ACA (¶¶1.1, 23) and considered the provisions of the Limitation Act, and their applicability and exclusion under Section 34(3) of the ACA, in light of Supreme Court decisions (¶17). The Supreme Court reiterated that delay beyond the 30 days prescribed in the proviso to Section 34(3) cannot be condoned by recourse to Section 5 of the Limitation Act, the scheme of the ACA excluding its applicability (¶18.2).The Supreme Court reiterated that Section 12 of the Limitation Act applies to the computation of limitation under Section 34(3) of ACA. Accordingly, the day from which the three-month limitation period is reckoned must be excluded in terms of Section 12(1) (¶19).The Supreme Court reiterated that exclusion of applicability of Section 5 of the Limitation Act does not mean that Section 14 is also inapplicable to Section 34(3) of the ACA (¶21).The Supreme Court reiterated that Section 17 of Limitation Act is inapplicable to Section 34(3) of the ACA as its applicability would defer commencement to discovery of fraud or mistake, extending the period beyond the statutory 3 months plus 30 days period (¶22.1). Section 4 Applies Only to the “Prescribed Period”The Supreme Court while relying upon Assam Urban Water Supply & Sewerage Board v. Subhash Projects & Marketing Limited ((2012) 2 SCC 624) and State of West Bengal v. Rajpath Contractors and Engineers Ltd. ((2024) 7 SCC 257) held that while Section 4 of the Limitation Act applies to Section 34(3) of the ACA, it only benefits a party when the “prescribed period”, meaning the 3-month limitation period, expires on a court holiday. (¶¶35.1, 35.2) Section 4 does not come to the aid of the party when the 3-month limitation period expires on a working day and only the 30-day condonable period expires on a court holiday (¶35.3). Justice Mithal concurred on this factual application, noting in his opinion that since the prescribed 3-month period expired on a working day i.e 29.05.2022, the appellants could not claim the benefit of Section 4 (¶13 J. Mithal).Inapplicability of Section 10 of the General Clauses Act The Supreme Court held that Section 10 of GCA stands excluded by virtue of its proviso, which renders it inapplicable where the Limitation Act applies (¶35.4) and since it was established that Section 4 of the Limitation Act applies to Section 34 proceedings, Section 10 of the GCA stands legally excluded and cannot be simultaneously claimed by the appellant (¶33, ¶14 J. Mithal).Concerns regarding strict construction of Limitation ActThe Supreme Court expressed deep concern that this stringent construction of limitation statutes unduly curtails the precious remedy available to arbitrating parties (¶36). The Supreme Court noted that strictly equating the “prescribed period” only with the 3 months makes the additional 30-day period lose its efficacy entirely if it expires on a holiday, and further observed that it is for the Legislature to take note of this position and bring clarity (¶¶36.4-36.6). In a supporting opinion, Justice Mithal further highlighted that strict limitation periods lacking the general condonation benefits of Section 5 of the Limitation Act cause severe prejudice to genuine litigants, and further suggested lawmakers to ensure a uniform period of limitation across all statutes (¶¶18-21 J. Mithal).Prepared by Nikhil Kumar ChaubeyCentre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India

Title: URMILA DIXIT vs SUNIL SHARAN DIXITC.A. No. 10927/2024
